wines.name: Cote-Rotie La Mouline
wines.oak_regime: 42 months in new French oak barriques

taste.summary: The most feminine of Guigal's La La La trilogy. Cote Blonde Syrah co-fermented with 11% Viognier gives a heady floral and red-fruit profile with silky tannins.

pairings.why: The violet and red-fruit profile mirrors the sauce; fine tannins integrate with duck fat.

pairings.dish: Lamb rack with herbs de Provence
pairings.why: Classic Rhone pairing; the wine's pepper note echoes the herbs.
The floral perfume and earthiness complement truffle aromas.

history.summary: Marcel Guigal first vinified La Mouline separately from the Cote Blonde in 1966. The practice of extended 42-month aging in new oak barrique began in the early 1980s, transforming the wine into one of the most coveted bottles in France.

milestones.event: First separate vinification of La Mouline by Marcel Guigal
42-month new-oak aging regime introduced across all three La La La cuvees

wines.description: Guigal's most seductive single-vineyard Cote-Rotie, co-fermented with 11% Viognier from the Cote Blonde. Floral, silky, and extraordinarily long-lived.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie La Landonne
wines.oak_regime: 42 months in new French oak barriques

taste.summary: The most powerful and structured of the trilogy. Pure Syrah from the Cote Brune iron-rich soils delivers a wine of granite-and-iron intensity that demands decades.

pairings.why: The wine's iron minerality and dense tannins demand richly marbled beef.
Game intensity matches La Landonne's dark, smoky fruit register.
Nutty crystalline cheese softens the wine's firmest tannins.

history.summary: Vinified separately from the Cote Brune lieu-dit beginning in 1975. 100% Syrah from iron-rich schist produces the most tannic and mineral of the Guigal single-vineyard trilogy.

milestones.event: First vintage of La Landonne produced separately by Marcel Guigal
La Turque added to complete the La La La trilogy

wines.description: 100% Cote Brune Syrah with iron-mineral structure and extraordinary ageing potential. The most powerful wine in Guigal's single-vineyard trilogy.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie La Turque
wines.oak_regime: 42 months in new French oak barriques

taste.summary: La Turque bridges La Mouline's floral elegance and La Landonne's iron power with exotic spice, dark chocolate, and 7% co-fermented Viognier.

pairings.why: The exotic spice and dark fruit match gamey venison perfectly.
Chocolate and earthy notes create harmony with black truffle.
The wine's exotic spice register complements the pigeon's richness.

history.summary: The last of the three single-vineyard Guigal cuvees, La Turque was first produced in 1985. It derives from a plot on the Cote Brune with some Cote Blonde influence, co-fermented with a small percentage of Viognier.

milestones.event: First vintage of La Turque completes the Guigal single-vineyard trilogy

wines.description: The youngest of Guigal's La La La trio, bridging floral elegance and iron power with exotic spice and 7% co-fermented Viognier from the Cote Brune.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Chateau d'Ampuis
wines.oak_regime: 36 months in new and used French oak barriques

taste.summary: An assemblage from seven prized Cote-Rotie lieux-dits, Chateau d'Ampuis offers an accessible entry point into Guigal's single-vineyard philosophy with genuine complexity.

pairings.why: Smoky pepper notes and black olive tapenade mirror the wine's character.
Slow-braised beef amplifies the dark fruit and spice intensity.
Earthy lentils and rich duck fat meet the wine's mineral backbone.

history.summary: First produced in 1995 after Guigal acquired the historic Chateau d'Ampuis property. Blends fruit from seven prestigious lieux-dits to create a wine between the single-vineyard trilogy and the appellation blend.

milestones.event: First vintage of Chateau d'Ampuis following Guigal's acquisition of the historic chateau

wines.description: Assemblage from seven Cote-Rotie lieux-dits, introduced after Guigal's acquisition of the historic chateau in 1995. More accessible than the La La La wines but equally complex.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Brune et Blonde
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak casks

taste.summary: The benchmark Cote-Rotie entry from Guigal, blending parcels from both Cote Brune and Cote Blonde. A terroir-honest introduction to the appellation at approachable price.

pairings.why: Medium-weight fruit and pepper pair perfectly with classic French roast chicken.
Peppery spice and the wine's violet and smoke notes are natural complements.
Rich creamy potato gratin loves a pepper-forward medium-bodied Syrah.

history.summary: The Guigal Brune et Blonde is the appellation's classic entry wine, blending fruit from both slopes. It has introduced Cote-Rotie to generations of wine drinkers since Etienne Guigal founded the house.

milestones.event: Etienne Guigal founds the house in Ampuis
First vintage of Brune et Blonde appellation blend

wines.description: The reference appellation-level Cote-Rotie from Guigal, blending Cote Brune and Cote Blonde parcels for a peppery, medium-structured Syrah. The most accessible route into the appellation.

wines.name: Condrieu La Doriane
wines.oak_regime: 8 months in small French oak barrels, 50% new

taste.summary: Guigal's top single-vineyard Condrieu from the Coteau de Condrieu plots. Rich, opulent Viognier at its most decadent with exceptional concentration.

pairings.why: The wine's richness and apricot notes are the classic match for foie gras.
The floral and stone-fruit character elevates a creamy shellfish bisque.
Viognier's stone fruit and creaminess are a textbook white asparagus pairing.

history.summary: La Doriane was first produced in 1994 as a selection of Guigal's finest Condrieu terroirs. Named by Marcel Guigal, it represents the house's most ambitious single-vineyard white wine.

milestones.event: First vintage of La Doriane Condrieu as a separate selection

wines.description: Guigal's flagship single-vineyard Condrieu, first made in 1994. 100% Viognier of exceptional aromatic intensity and textural richness from the Coteau de Condrieu.

wines.name: Ermitage Ex-Voto Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 30 months in new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Guigal's flagship Hermitage from a specific parcel on the granite hill of Tain. Dense, mineral 100% Syrah with black fruit, leather and oriental spice complexity.

pairings.dish: Roast leg of lamb with garlic and rosemary
pairings.why: The classic Hermitage and Rhone lamb pairing; mineral Syrah and lamb fat are natural companions.
Game intensity and the wine's granite structure create a compelling partnership.
Slow-cooked collagen-rich beef matches the wine's concentrated dark fruit register.

history.summary: Ex-Voto was first produced in 2007 from a specific parcel of old Syrah vines on the Hermitage hill. The name means votive offering. It is Guigal's most concentrated and expensive Hermitage.

milestones.event: First vintage of Ermitage Ex-Voto rouge

wines.description: Guigal's prestige Hermitage from a single parcel of old Syrah vines on the granite hill of Tain. First made in 2007, Ex-Voto delivers Northern Rhone Syrah at its most concentrated.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Vignes de l'Hospice
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in new and used French oak

taste.summary: Old-vine Saint-Joseph Syrah from historic Hospice plots in Tournon. Complex black fruit nose with delicate oak, powerful and elegant on the palate.

pairings.why: Smoked olive aromas and the wine's dark fruit find resonance with braised pork.
Fresh herb notes in the wine mirror the lamb's seasoning.
Earthy mineral notes complement mushroom and lentil earthiness.

history.summary: The Vignes de l'Hospice is from plots belonging to the Hospices de Tournon in the historic Saint-Joseph zone. Guigal manages and vinifies separately to produce one of the appellation's finest single-vineyard expressions.

milestones.event: Guigal begins separate vinification of the Hospice parcel in Saint-Joseph

wines.description: Guigal's prestige Saint-Joseph from the historic Hospice de Tournon plots. Old-vine Syrah on granite with the smoky mineral character that defines Northern Rhone reds.

wines.name: Ermitage Le Pavillon
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new French oak barriques (300L)

taste.summary: Chapoutier's crown jewel, from a single parcel on the granite hill of Hermitage. Biodynamically farmed old-vine Syrah.

pairings.why: The Northern Rhone benchmark food pairing; mineral Syrah and roast lamb are inseparable.
The wine's density and mineral quality meet gamey venison head-on.
Puff pastry richness and mushroom duxelles match the wine's complexity.

history.summary: Le Pavillon was first produced in 1989 when Michel Chapoutier began the biodynamic conversion of the Hermitage hill parcels. The wine is made from a single lieu-dit Les Bessards on the southern granite face of the Hermitage hill.

milestones.event: First vintage of Le Pavillon as Chapoutier begins biodynamic conversion of Hermitage
Full biodynamic certification achieved for Hermitage parcels

wines.description: Chapoutier's flagship Hermitage rouge from a single parcel on Les Bessards. Biodynamically farmed old Syrah on granite. One of France's most age-worthy reds.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Ermitage L'Ermite Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new French oak demi-muids

taste.summary: From the L'Ermite parcel at the summit of the Hermitage hill, the most mineral and austere of Chapoutier's single-parcel reds. Demands the longest cellaring.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with anchovy and rosemary crust
pairings.why: The salt and mineral character in the wine finds an anchor in the anchovy-lamb combination.
Rustic fare with enough depth to meet the wine's mineral concentration.
Firm, nutty cheese softens the wine's muscular tannins.

history.summary: L'Ermite is named for the hermit who historically lived on the Hermitage hill. The parcel sits at the highest point of the hill and produces the most mineral of Chapoutier's single-vineyard Hermitage reds.

milestones.event: First vintage of L'Ermite from the summit parcel of the Hermitage hill

wines.description: From the summit parcel of the Hermitage hill, L'Ermite is the most mineral and austere of Chapoutier's single-parcel reds. Demands patient cellaring of 12-35 years.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Ermitage Le Meal Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new French oak demi-muids

taste.finish: very long, velvety with dark spice
taste.summary: Le Meal is the most hedonistic and velvety of Chapoutier's Hermitage single-parcel reds, from mid-slope schist and granite. More immediately accessible than Le Pavillon or L'Ermite.

pairings.dish: Beef tenderloin with black truffle sauce
pairings.why: The wine's velvety texture and chocolate notes meet truffle-enriched beef naturally.
Luxurious poultry and the wine's exotic spice register create a special occasion pairing.
The wine's cocoa and spice character bridges into rich dark chocolate dessert.

history.summary: Le Meal comes from mid-slope parcels on the Hermitage hill above Tain-l'Hermitage. It is considered the most accessible of the three single-parcel reds in its youth while gaining great complexity with age.

milestones.event: First vintage of Le Meal as part of Chapoutier's Selections Parcellaires program

wines.description: The most velvety and hedonistic of Chapoutier's Hermitage single-parcel reds, from mid-slope parcels. Biodynamic old-vine Syrah with dark fruit and chocolate density.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Ermitage Monier de la Sizeranne
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak casks

taste.summary: The historic assemblage Hermitage from Chapoutier, named for Monier de la Sizeranne who codified Hermitage in the 19th century. Blends multiple parcels for a complete expression of the hill.

pairings.why: The wine's olive and herb character and tapenade on lamb are a Rhone classic.
Dark fruit and earth mineral in the wine match beef and earthy mushrooms.
Rustic bean and duck casserole needs a structured, herb-forward red.

history.summary: Monier de la Sizeranne is named for the botanist and Hermitage historian who mapped the hill's terroirs in the 19th century. It is the historic assemblage Hermitage from the Chapoutier house.

milestones.event: Chapoutier first labels the assemblage Hermitage under this historic name

wines.description: The historic assemblage Hermitage rouge from Chapoutier, named for the 19th-century botanist who mapped the hill. Biodynamic multi-parcel Syrah at a more accessible price than the single-vineyard trio.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Ermitage De l'Oree Blanc
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in new French oak barriques

taste.summary: One of the world's most profound white wines. 100% Marsanne from the De l'Oree parcel of the Hermitage hill, biodynamically farmed, producing liquid gold that evolves magnificently for decades.

pairings.why: The wine's waxy richness and mineral depth are in harmony with lobster cream.
The wine's honey and hazelnut register magnifies white truffle aromatics.
Nutty oxidative characters in aged Comté mirror the wine's evolved hazelnut notes.

history.summary: De l'Oree was first produced in 1990 from a specific parcel of old Marsanne vines on the Hermitage hill. Marsanne's tendency to close down after 3-5 years and re-emerge after 10-15 years makes De l'Oree a wine for patient collectors.

milestones.event: First vintage of De l'Oree as a separate single-parcel white Hermitage

wines.description: 100% Marsanne from a single Hermitage parcel, biodynamically farmed. Waxy honey and mineral complexity that can develop for 30 years.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Chante-Alouette Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Partial barrel fermentation, minimal new oak

taste.summary: The historic assemblage white Hermitage from Chapoutier. 100% Marsanne unified across multiple parcels, producing a richly textured white that is more accessible than De l'Oree.

pairings.why: The wine's round stone fruit and creamy texture mirrors the butter sauce.
Cream-based veal stew is elevated by Marsanne's rich textured white fruit.
The wine's almond and peach notes are a classic match for white asparagus.

history.summary: Chante-Alouette is one of the oldest named bottlings in the Northern Rhone, dating to the mid-19th century. The name translates as singing lark. It is the classic assemblage white Hermitage from the Chapoutier house.

milestones.event: Chante-Alouette established as the historic named white Hermitage from Chapoutier

wines.description: The historic named white Hermitage from Chapoutier dating to the 19th century. 100% Marsanne assemblage with rich stone fruit, beeswax, and creamy mineral texture.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Condrieu Invitare
wines.oak_regime: 8 months in new and used French oak barrels

taste.summary: Chapoutier's Condrieu from the Coteau de Chery parcel. Biodynamically farmed Viognier with great aromatic intensity and rich tropical-fruit texture.

pairings.why: Tropical fruit and citrus in the wine echo the langoustine preparation.
Saffron's aromatic intensity meets Viognier's tropical-floral register.
The wine's richness and fruit balance the herbal Mediterranean preparation.

history.summary: Invitare draws on parcels from the Coteau de Chery in Condrieu. Under biodynamic farming, Chapoutier produces a Viognier of aromatic purity and rich texture.

milestones.event: First vintage of Invitare Condrieu from the Coteau de Chery parcels

wines.description: Chapoutier's Condrieu from the Coteau de Chery, biodynamically farmed. Lush tropical-fruit Viognier with great aromatic intensity.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage Les Varonniers
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new French oak barriques

taste.summary: Chapoutier's single-vineyard Les Varonniers Crozes, from old Syrah on the granite terraces directly below the Hermitage hill. Approaches Hermitage in quality at a fraction of the price.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with herbes de Provence
pairings.why: The wine's spice and dark fruit align with herb-crusted lamb.
Iron mineral and black fruit pair with charred, marbled beef.
Black cherry in the wine harmonises with the duck cherry glaze.

history.summary: Les Varonniers is Chapoutier's prestige Crozes-Hermitage, from a single parcel of old Syrah vines on the granite terraces closest to the Hermitage hill in Larnage.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Varonniers from the granite terraces above Larnage

wines.description: Chapoutier's prestige single-vineyard Crozes-Hermitage, from old Syrah on granite terraces below the Hermitage hill. Biodynamic farming and near-Hermitage quality.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Cornas Les Arenes
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak demi-muids

taste.summary: Chapoutier's Cornas from the Les Arenes parcel on granite. Biodynamically farmed old Syrah with the smoky, iron-edged character that defines Cornas AOC.

pairings.why: Black olive and smoked meat in the wine resonate with grilled lamb and tapenade.
Game bird with rich sauce needs the wine's iron mineral backbone.
The wine's rustic tannins are softened by crystalline sheep's-milk cheese.

history.summary: Les Arenes is Chapoutier's single-parcel Cornas from the granite terraces of the village. Biodynamically farmed, it represents the house's interpretation of Cornas' historic granite-terroir character.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Arenes as Chapoutier's Cornas AOC selection

wines.description: Chapoutier's Cornas from the Les Arenes granite parcel. Biodynamic old-vine Syrah with the iron-mineral, smoked-olive character defining Cornas AOC.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Granits Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new and used French oak barriques

taste.summary: Chapoutier's flagship Saint-Joseph, from old granite parcels in the northern part of the appellation. Biodynamic Syrah with concentration and terroir focus.

pairings.why: The wine's pepper, herbs, and dark fruit match the richness of roasted shoulder.
Raw beef's mineral and fat are complemented by the wine's granite tannins.
Generous marbled beef steak needs a wine with structure and concentration.

history.summary: Les Granits is a single-parcel Saint-Joseph from old Syrah vines on granite soils in the appellation's most favoured northern terroir. It is Chapoutier's most ambitious Saint-Joseph.

milestones.event: First vintage of Granits Rouge Saint-Joseph from the northern granite terroirs

wines.description: Chapoutier's prestige Saint-Joseph from old Syrah vines on northern granite terroirs. Biodynamic farming and concentrated pepper-mineral character.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Hermitage La Chapelle
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in French oak casks and barriques

pairings.dish: Roast leg of lamb with garlic
pairings.why: The most classic Northern Rhone pairing: mineral Syrah and roast lamb.
Rich gamey hare stew needs a wine of equal intensity and mineral complexity.

pairings.dish: Grilled cote de boeuf with bone marrow
pairings.why: The wine's structure and dark fruit match the richness of aged beef and marrow.

history.summary: Named for the Saint-Christophe chapel on the Hermitage hill, La Chapelle has been produced since the Jaboulet house was founded in 1834. The 1961 vintage remains the most-cited historic Jaboulet release.

milestones.event: Paul Jaboulet Aine founds the house; La Chapelle is the founding cuvee
La Chapelle 1961 released; one of the house's signature historic vintages

wines.description: Named for the chapel on the Hermitage hill, La Chapelle is one of the Northern Rhone's most historic wines. 100% Syrah with legendary ageing potential from the house founded in 1834.

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage Domaine de Thalabert
wines.oak_regime: 12-18 months in used French oak casks

pairings.dish: Roast pork with olives and capers
pairings.why: Smoked olive character in the wine resonates with the pork and olive preparation.
Classic Northern Rhone pairing; the pepper and dark fruit match well.
The wine's medium tannins and savoury quality complement a French charcuterie selection.

history.summary: Domaine de Thalabert has been the reference Crozes-Hermitage estate wine from the Jaboulet house since the early 1970s. The domaine is one of Crozes-Hermitage's most recognised sites.

milestones.event: Jaboulet begins separate estate bottling of Domaine de Thalabert

wines.description: Jaboulet's estate Crozes-Hermitage, the benchmark for the appellation since the early 1970s. Medium-weight Syrah with smoked olive and pepper at an accessible price.

wines.name: Hermitage Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in large French oak foudres and demi-muids, minimal new oak

taste.finish: very long, iron and mineral driven

pairings.dish: Roast leg of lamb a la ficelle
pairings.why: The most reverential Rhone pairing; Syrah from the Hermitage hill and whole roast lamb.

pairings.dish: Braised hare with cognac and blood sauce
pairings.why: Rich, intense game sauce needs the mineral depth and concentration of Chave.

pairings.dish: Grilled Cote de boeuf from Salers cattle
pairings.why: The wine's granitic tannins and dark fruit pair with the finest French beef.

history.summary: The Chave family has owned vines on the Hermitage hill since 1481, making it one of the longest continuous viticultural families in France. Jean-Louis Chave assembles multiple lieux-dits each year to produce an Hermitage of extraordinary complexity.

milestones.event: First recorded Chave ownership of Hermitage hill parcels
Gerard Chave takes over from his father, beginning the modern era of the domaine
Jean-Louis Chave joins his father Gerard; the domaine name becomes Domaine Jean-Louis Chave

wines.description: The Chave family's flagship Hermitage, assembled from multiple lieux-dits on the hill. 500-year family ownership.

wines.name: Hermitage Blanc
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in older French oak foudres

pairings.why: The wine's waxy richness and mineral depth are an ideal partner for butter-finished turbot.
Earthy morels and delicate sweetbreads match the wine's hazelnut and mineral character.
Rich shellfish and cream amplify the wine's textural generosity.

history.summary: Like the rouge, Chave's Hermitage blanc has been produced across centuries from white parcels on the Hermitage hill. The blend of Marsanne and Roussanne is a traditional Hermitage white formulation.

milestones.event: Chave family begins cultivating white-grape lieux-dits on the Hermitage hill

wines.description: The Chave Hermitage blanc, a Marsanne and Roussanne assemblage from multiple white parcels on the hill. Among the world's most age-worthy white wines.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 12-18 months in older French oak foudres

pairings.why: The wine's violet, pepper, and mineral character perfectly complement herb-marinated lamb.
Silky tannins and wild fruit pair beautifully with roasted pigeon.
Earthy lentils and smoked pork echo the wine's mineral and pepper register.

history.summary: The Chave family's Saint-Joseph comes from parcels in Mauves, part of the historical heartland of the appellation. Minimal intervention and older oak preserve the pure granite-terroir expression.

milestones.event: Gerard Chave begins separate bottling of Saint-Joseph from the Mauves parcels

wines.description: The Chave family's Saint-Joseph from prime Mauves parcels. Minimal-intervention Syrah on granite with the violet and pepper character of a benchmark Northern Rhone red.

wines.name: Cornas
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in old French oak foudres and casks

pairings.why: Black olive aromatics in the wine find resonance with olive-stuffed lamb.

pairings.dish: Grilled beef rib with bone marrow
pairings.why: The wine's density and mineral quality demands the richest cut of beef.
Long-braised collagen-rich beef suits the wine's concentrated mineral tannins.

history.summary: Auguste Clape founded the domaine in 1955 when Cornas was an obscure appellation. His insistence on old vine Syrah, traditional winemaking, and late release helped define Cornas as a world-class appellation.

milestones.event: Auguste Clape founds the domaine in Cornas
Pierre Clape joins his father; the domaine begins to gain international recognition
Pierre-Marie Clape (3rd generation) takes over winemaking

wines.description: The reference Cornas, produced by the family that defined the appellation. Old-vine Syrah on granite with iron-mineral concentration and traditional old-oak aging.

wines.name: Cornas Renaissance
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in used French oak casks

pairings.why: The wine's lighter grip and dark fruit suit charcuterie perfectly.
Casual lamb preparations match the approachable Cornas character.
Earthy flavors and moderate tannins pair naturally.

history.summary: Renaissance is the second label of the Clape domaine, introduced to handle younger-vine fruit that is excellent but not up to the concentration standard of the flagship Cornas.

milestones.event: Clape introduces Renaissance as a second cuvee from younger-vine parcels

wines.description: Clape's approachable second label from younger-vine Cornas. Maintains the family's terroir honesty at a more accessible price and earlier drinking window.

wines.name: Saint-Peray
wines.oak_regime: Fermented and aged in older French oak foudres

pairings.why: The wine's richness and mineral character are ideal for firm-fleshed white fish.
The Marsanne's textural richness mirrors cream-based poultry preparations.
Beeswax and almond notes complement white asparagus beautifully.

history.summary: The Clape family has produced Saint-Peray alongside Cornas for generations. The wine comes from old Marsanne vines in Saint-Peray, vinified in the same traditional manner as the reds.

milestones.event: Clape begins bottling Saint-Peray from family Marsanne parcels

wines.description: Clape's rare still Saint-Peray from old Marsanne vines. Traditional foudre vinification produces a rich, mineral white that rewards cellaring.

wines.name: Cornas Reynard
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in old French oak foudres and demi-muids

pairings.dish: Roast leg of lamb with anchovy and garlic
pairings.why: The wine's iron intensity and dark fruit meet the richest lamb preparations.

pairings.dish: Wild boar with lentils and truffle
pairings.why: Gamey intensity and earthy truffle match the wine's mineral concentration.
Local artisan cheese from the Ardeche is the natural companion for this Cornas.

history.summary: Thierry Allemand took over centenarian-vine parcels in Cornas in 1982 and produced his first Reynard in 1985. The wine is made from vines over 80 years old in the historic Reynard lieu-dit and has become one of the most coveted natural wines in France.

milestones.event: Thierry Allemand acquires his centenarian-vine Cornas parcels
First vintage of Reynard from the oldest vine parcel

wines.description: From centenarian Cornas vines, Reynard is Thierry Allemand's cult cuvee. Zero-intervention old-vine Syrah with savage iron-mineral intensity and extraordinary ageing potential.

wines.name: Cornas Chaillot
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in old French oak foudres and demi-muids

pairings.dish: Grilled lamb rack with herbs de Provence
pairings.why: The wine's fresh herb and violet notes pair with herb-crusted lamb.
Pepper-forward Syrah and duck breast are a natural Rhone combination.
Local charcuterie from the Ardeche pairs with this Cornas in the most natural way.

history.summary: Chaillot uses fruit from the younger-vine plots in the Chaillot lieu-dit. It was produced alongside Reynard from Allemand's first vintage to offer a slightly more accessible version of his zero-intervention Cornas philosophy.

milestones.event: First vintage of Chaillot produced alongside Reynard from Chaillot lieu-dit

wines.description: Allemand's younger-vine Cornas from Chaillot lieu-dit. Same zero-intervention approach as Reynard but with more vibrant fruit and slightly earlier accessibility.

wines.name: Cornas Granit 30
wines.oak_regime: 12-16 months in used French oak barriques and demi-muids

pairings.why: The wine's vibrant fruit and pepper notes match Middle-Eastern lamb spicing.
Medium-bodied Syrah with its earthy mineral notes pair naturally with pork and mushroom.
Value Cornas with its fresh dark fruit is perfect alongside a classic charcuterie selection.

history.summary: Vincent Paris founded his domaine in 1997 after working at Clape and other Northern Rhone producers. Granit 30 uses vines approximately 30 years old on Cornas granite, priced to compete with entry-level Crozes.

milestones.event: Vincent Paris founds the domaine in Cornas after apprenticeships in the Northern Rhone

wines.description: Vincent Paris's entry-level Cornas from approximately 30-year-old vines on granite. Vibrant, approachable Syrah offering genuine Cornas terroir at an accessible price.

wines.name: Cornas Granit 60
wines.oak_regime: 16-20 months in used French oak barriques and foudres

pairings.dish: Roast rack of lamb with garlic and thyme
pairings.why: Classic Northern Rhone pairing; the wine's mineral depth and dark fruit match slow-roasted lamb.
Game and mushroom earthiness meet the wine's mineral concentration.
Provencale beef stew and the wine's olive and dark fruit character are complementary.

history.summary: Granit 60 uses fruit from the oldest vines in the Paris domaine, approximately 60 years old. The increased vine age produces markedly more concentration and mineral depth than Granit 30.

milestones.event: First vintage of Granit 60 from the domaine's oldest vine parcels

wines.description: Vincent Paris's prestige Cornas from 60-year-old vines on granite. Concentrated, iron-mineral Syrah approaching the quality of the appellation's elite at a more accessible price.

wines.name: Cornas La Geynale
wines.oak_regime: 18-22 months in new and used French oak barriques

pairings.dish: Bone-in rib of beef with roasted marrow
pairings.why: The wine's iron mineral tannins demand the richest, most flavourful beef cut.
Rich gamey hare stew rewards a deeply mineral Cornas.
Soft creamy cheese softens the wine's firm tannins elegantly.

history.summary: La Geynale was first produced in 2003 as Vincent Paris's top single-vineyard cuvee. It comes from a specific terroir in Cornas that Paris identified as having the greatest mineral potential on his estate.

milestones.event: First vintage of La Geynale as Paris's single-vineyard prestige cuvee

wines.description: Vincent Paris's single-vineyard prestige Cornas, first produced in 2003. Deeply mineral granite-terroir Syrah representing the full potential of this rising Cornas producer.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie
wines.oak_regime: 18-24 months in used French oak foudres and demi-muids

pairings.dish: Roast duck with black cherry sauce
pairings.why: The wine's violet and dark cherry character embraces duck and cherry.
Classic French lamb stew is natural ground for mineral Cote-Rotie.

pairings.dish: Lentils du Puy with smoked pork
pairings.why: Earthy lentils and smoked pork echo the wine's savoury mineral quality.

history.summary: Jean-Paul and Jean-Luc Jamet took over their father's vines in Ampuis and developed a philosophy of assembling all parcels into a single Cote-Rotie of exceptional complexity. The domaine is admired worldwide for this approach to Northern Rhone terroir.

milestones.event: Jamet brothers found the domaine and begin separate estate Cote-Rotie bottling

wines.description: Jamet's assemblage Cote-Rotie from multiple parcels on Cote Brune and Cote Blonde. One estate, one wine: extraordinary depth and mineral complexity from Ampuis.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie La Landonne
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak barriques and demi-muids

pairings.why: Iron mineral tannins and the finest French beef are a natural match.
The wine's weight and mineral character meets slow-cooked game.
Nutty aged cheese softens the wine's powerful tannins.

history.summary: Rene Rostaing inherited parcels on La Landonne from his father-in-law Albert Dervieux and began the separate bottling in 1971. The ferrous, iron-rich schist of the Cote Brune defines the wine's mineral character.

milestones.event: First separate La Landonne bottling from the Cote Brune parcels

wines.description: Rostaing's La Landonne from the Cote Brune's iron-rich schist. Pure Syrah with ferrous mineral intensity and exceptional ageing potential.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Cote Blonde
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak barriques

pairings.why: The wine's elegance and floral character pair beautifully with mild poultry.
Aromatic herb-crusted lamb is complemented by the wine's floral lift.
The wine's elegance and co-fermented Viognier aromas work with pastry-encased salmon.

history.summary: The Cote Blonde parcels of Rostaing include the historic Vialliere site. The wine contrasts La Landonne's power with elegance and aromatics from the mica-rich gneiss terroir.

milestones.event: Rostaing begins producing Cote Blonde separately from the lighter-soil parcels

wines.description: Rostaing's Cote Blonde Cote-Rotie co-fermented with 8% Viognier. More floral and elegant than La Landonne, from mica-rich gneiss soils on the milder slope.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Ampodium
wines.oak_regime: 14 months in used French oak barriques and larger casks

pairings.why: Moderate-weight Cote-Rotie and spiced lamb sausage are a perfect casual match.
Fresh herbal Syrah and herb-roasted chicken are natural companions.
Medium-weight Cote-Rotie is perfect for an earthy mushroom egg dish.

history.summary: Ampodium is the appellation-level Cote-Rotie from Rostaing, introduced to offer a more accessible entry point than the single-vineyard cuvees. It blends parcels from both slopes.

milestones.event: Ampodium introduced as the estate Cote-Rotie entry-level from Rostaing

wines.description: Rostaing's multi-parcel entry Cote-Rotie, combining fruit from Cote Brune and Cote Blonde at an accessible price. The introduction to this prestigious appellation.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Belle Helene
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in new French oak barriques (100% new oak)

taste.finish: very long, dark spice and mineral
taste.summary: Ogier's ultra-premium single-vine-parcel Cote-Rotie from the Lancement site, aged in 100% new oak. Among the most expressive and sought-after modern Cote-Rotie wines.

pairings.why: The wine's new oak, dark fruit, and cedar match the luxury combination of beef and truffle in pastry.
The wine's power and concentration needs intensely marbled beef of equal richness.

pairings.dish: Pigeon royale with foie gras jus
pairings.why: Game bird and foie gras jus meet the wine's power and complexity head-on.

history.summary: Belle Helene was introduced in 1997 as Stephane Ogier's top single-vineyard Cote-Rotie. Named after a specific parcel on the Cote Brune, it uses 100% new oak and represents his most ambitious expression of the appellation.

milestones.event: First vintage of Belle Helene as Ogier's prestige single-vineyard cuvee

wines.description: Ogier's prestige single-vineyard Cote-Rotie from Lancement parcel, 100% new oak aged. Among the most powerful and concentrated Cote-Rotie bottlings produced today.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Lancement
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new and used French oak barriques

taste.summary: The entry-level single-vineyard Cote-Rotie from Ogier, assembling the Lancement parcel with both Cote Brune and Cote Blonde elements. More accessible than Belle Helene, but showing the same terroir precision.

pairings.why: Classic Northern Rhone pairing; concentrated dark fruit and mineral pair with lamb.
The wine's violet and cherry register resonates with duck and cherry preparations.
Hearty French preparation suits the wine's mineral and peppery character.

history.summary: Lancement is the domaine-level Cote-Rotie from Stephane Ogier, introduced at the same time as Belle Helene to provide a more accessible entry into the estate's wines.

milestones.event: Ogier introduces Lancement alongside Belle Helene as the estate Cote-Rotie

wines.description: Ogier's estate-level Cote-Rotie from the Lancement parcel. Concentrated dark fruit, mineral depth, and pepper spice at a more accessible price than Belle Helene.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Coteaux de Tupin
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak foudres

pairings.why: The wine's floral, peppery character pairs with herb-crusted lamb.
Medium-weight Cote-Rotie is excellent with roast chicken and Mediterranean flavors.
Classic Lyonnaise preparation suits the appellation and the wine's moderate weight.

history.summary: The Bonnefond brothers have farmed the Coteaux de Tupin sector of Cote-Rotie since 1980. Their wines are known for elegance and floral character, reflecting the slightly different geology of this southern portion of the appellation.

milestones.event: Bonnefond brothers establish their own estate in the Coteaux de Tupin

wines.description: The Bonnefond brothers' Cote-Rotie from the Coteaux de Tupin. Elegant, floral, approachable Syrah from a less-known sector of the appellation at reasonable prices.

wines.name: Condrieu Coteau de Vernon
wines.oak_regime: 9 months in French oak barrels, 20% new

taste.summary: From the oldest Viognier vines in Condrieu, the Coteau de Vernon is considered the appellation's greatest Viognier. Christine Vernay continues her father's legacy with this flagship single-vineyard wine.

pairings.dish: Seared foie gras with apricot chutney
pairings.why: The wine's apricot blossom and honey profile is the classic foie gras pairing.
The wine's richness and mineral depth elevate luxurious shellfish.
The classic Viognier and white asparagus pairing is at its most refined.

history.summary: Georges Vernay saved Condrieu from near-extinction in the 1970s when fewer than 2 hectares remained in production. The Coteau de Vernon, first produced in 1979, became the appellation's benchmark and a symbol of its revival.

milestones.event: Georges Vernay founds the domaine and begins replanting abandoned Condrieu parcels
First separate bottling of Coteau de Vernon from the oldest vines in the appellation
Christine Vernay takes over the domaine from her father

wines.description: From the oldest Viognier vines in Condrieu, the Coteau de Vernon is the appellation's benchmark. Christine Vernay continues her father's revival of this near-extinct appellation.

wines.name: Condrieu Les Terrasses de l'Empire
wines.oak_regime: 7 months in used French oak barrels

taste.summary: Vernay's entry-level Condrieu, showing the appellation's characteristic apricot and floral freshness in a more accessible and earlier-drinking style than Coteau de Vernon.

pairings.why: The wine's floral and stone-fruit character pairs with saffron-scented scallops.
The wine's apricot notes find a natural food mirror in fruit-braised poultry.
The wine's weight and floral aromatics balance fried vegetable dishes.

history.summary: The Terrasses de l'Empire represents Vernay's more approachable Condrieu, sourced from the lower terraced parcels of the domaine. It provides an entry into the appellation style without the concentration of Coteau de Vernon.

milestones.event: First vintage of Terrasses de l'Empire as the estate entry Condrieu

wines.description: Vernay's entry Condrieu from the terraced lower parcels. Approachable floral and stone-fruit Viognier, more accessible than Coteau de Vernon.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Maison Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak barriques

taste.summary: Vernay's Cote-Rotie, a house better known for Condrieu but producing an elegant and honest Syrah. The small Viognier co-fermentation adds Condrieu-like floral notes.

pairings.why: Medium-weight peppery Syrah pairs with herbal roasted poultry.
The wine's herbs and pepper match Middle Eastern lamb preparations.
Medium-bodied Cote-Rotie suits the richness of duck pate.

history.summary: The Vernay domaine expanded from Condrieu into Cote-Rotie with the Maison Rouge bottling, producing a floral and approachable Syrah that reflects the house's Viognier expertise through a small co-fermentation.

milestones.event: First vintage of Maison Rouge as Vernay's entry into the Cote-Rotie appellation

wines.description: Vernay's Cote-Rotie, co-fermented with 4% Viognier for a floral, approachable character. An elegant Syrah from a house better known for Condrieu.

wines.name: Condrieu Les Chaillets Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: 10 months in French oak barrels, 30% new

taste.summary: Old-vine Viognier from the Les Chaillets site above Chavanay. Cuilleron's classic Condrieu, renowned for its lush, tropical-influenced aromatic richness and old-vine depth.

pairings.why: The wine's tropical fruit and mango notes mirror the salsa preparation.
Viognier's tropical aromatics complement aromatic Asian-inspired fish preparations.
Classic Northern Rhone pairing: Viognier and foie gras create an aromatic harmony.

history.summary: Cuilleron founded his domaine in 1987 and quickly established Les Chaillets as his benchmark Condrieu. The old-vine Viognier from this site is among the most consistent producers in the appellation.

milestones.event: Yves Cuilleron founds the domaine; Les Chaillets is the founding Condrieu cuvee

wines.description: Cuilleron's flagship old-vine Condrieu from Les Chaillets. Lush, tropical Viognier with aromatic intensity and old-vine depth from the Chavanay terroirs.

wines.name: Condrieu La Petite Cote
wines.oak_regime: 8 months in used French oak barrels

taste.summary: Cuilleron's entry-level Condrieu, lighter in weight and more delicate than Les Chaillets. Ideal for early drinking, showing the appellation's floral character without heavy oak influence.

pairings.why: The wine's fresh floral character complements delicate fish and fennel preparations.
Classic Viognier and asparagus pairing with a lighter-style wine for easy occasions.
Medium-bodied white with earthy mineral notes pairs naturally with mushroom risotto.

history.summary: La Petite Cote is Cuilleron's approachable entry Condrieu, introduced to showcase the appellation's floral freshness without the old-vine concentration of Les Chaillets.

milestones.event: First vintage of La Petite Cote as Cuilleron's entry-level Condrieu

wines.description: Cuilleron's approachable entry Condrieu, showing the appellation's floral freshness in a lighter, more immediately accessible style.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph L'Amarybelle Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 14 months in used French oak barriques

taste.summary: Cuilleron's flagship Saint-Joseph rouge from old Syrah vines on granite. A serious, concentrated Northern Rhone Syrah that showcases the Saint-Joseph appellation at its finest.

pairings.dish: Lamb daube with herbs de Provence
pairings.why: Provencale lamb stew and concentrated Saint-Joseph Syrah are a natural pairing.
Earthy lentils and smoked pork find resonance in the wine's mineral and herb character.

pairings.dish: Duck breast with olives and capers
pairings.why: The wine's olive notes echo the preparation while dark fruit matches duck.

history.summary: L'Amarybelle is Cuilleron's prestige Saint-Joseph rouge from old vines on granite terraces in the northern part of the appellation. It showcases the serious red-wine potential of Saint-Joseph.

milestones.event: First vintage of L'Amarybelle as Cuilleron's flagship Saint-Joseph rouge

wines.description: Cuilleron's flagship Saint-Joseph rouge from old Syrah vines on granite. Concentrated, peppery Syrah showcasing Saint-Joseph at its most serious.

wines.name: Condrieu Coteau Chery
wines.oak_regime: 8 months in used French oak barrels

pairings.why: The appellation's classic pairing; the wine's honey and stone fruit lift foie gras.
The wine's aromatic intensity and creaminess are amplified by rich shellfish soup.

pairings.dish: Roasted white asparagus with truffle oil
pairings.why: Coteau Chery's depth and floral character elevate asparagus and truffle.

history.summary: Andre Perret founded his domaine in 1984 specifically to work the Coteau Chery, one of Condrieu's historic premier sites. The wine is from granite terraces above Chavanay, considered one of the finest Viognier positions in the appellation.

milestones.event: Andre Perret founds the domaine, focusing on the Coteau Chery as the flagship

wines.description: Perret's flagship Condrieu from one of the appellation's premier sites, Coteau Chery. Rich, honeyed Viognier with mineral depth from granite terraces above Chavanay.

wines.name: Condrieu Clos Chanson
wines.oak_regime: 7 months in used French oak barrels

pairings.why: The wine's light floral texture pairs gently with steamed white fish and fennel.

pairings.dish: Goat cheese salad with apricot vinaigrette
pairings.why: The wine's apricot notes echo the vinaigrette while the acidity cuts the goat cheese.
Mineral Viognier and cream-sauced mussels are a natural pairing.

history.summary: Clos Chanson is a small enclosed vineyard parcel producing a slightly more restrained expression of Condrieu than the Coteau Chery site, vinified as a fresh, floral single-vineyard bottling.

milestones.event: First vintage of Clos Chanson as a separate single-vineyard Condrieu

wines.description: Perret's single-walled-vineyard Condrieu. Slightly more linear and fresh than Coteau Chery, with the appellation's characteristic floral and stone-fruit aromatics.

wines.name: Condrieu Le Grand Vallon
wines.oak_regime: 10 months in new and used French oak barrels

pairings.why: The wine's tropical richness and exotic spice are amplified by the vanilla butter.
The wine's honey and stone fruit work with the sweet-savory contrast of foie gras.
Tropical elements in the wine echo the mango and coriander in the lobster preparation.

history.summary: Francois Villard left a career as a chef to found his domaine in 1991. His Condrieu wines quickly gained recognition for their intensity and terroir focus. Le Grand Vallon is his benchmark Condrieu.

milestones.event: Francois Villard founds the domaine; Le Grand Vallon is established as the flagship Condrieu

wines.description: Villard's flagship Condrieu, known for exotic, tropical intensity. 100% Viognier with creamy oak texture and mineral spine from a former chef turned committed vigneron.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Le Grand Pompee
wines.oak_regime: 14 months in new and used French oak barriques

pairings.why: The wine's concentration and pepper spice mirror the complexity of romesco with lamb.
The wine's mineral and spice notes bridge to earthy mushroom and truffle.
Rich duck confit is elevated by the wine's dark fruit and mineral character.

history.summary: Le Grand Pompee is Villard's prestige Saint-Joseph rouge, from old Syrah vines in the granitic heart of the appellation. The name references the grand Pompee Roman amphitheater imagery of the Rhone Valley.

milestones.event: First vintage of Le Grand Pompee as Villard's flagship Saint-Joseph rouge

wines.description: Villard's prestige Saint-Joseph from old Syrah vines on granite. Concentrated, spiced, and mineral, reflecting the former chef's ambition in the vineyard.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie Esprit de Blonde
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new and used French oak barriques

pairings.dish: Guinea fowl with tarragon cream sauce
pairings.why: The wine's elegance and floral character suits mild poultry in cream.
The floral and pepper notes echo the herb crust on the lamb.
The wine's Viognier apricot character bridges to the apricot hollandaise.

history.summary: Pierre Gaillard founded his domaine in 1987 and by 1989 was producing the Esprit de Blonde from his Cote Blonde parcels. The wine expresses the lighter, more aromatic character of the mica-gneiss Cote Blonde soils.

milestones.event: Pierre Gaillard founds the domaine in Malleval
First vintage of Esprit de Blonde from the Cote Blonde parcels

wines.description: Gaillard's Cote Blonde Cote-Rotie co-fermented with 10% Viognier. Floral, aromatic elegance from mica-gneiss soils, offering the lighter and perfumed side of the appellation.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Clos de Cuminal
wines.oak_regime: 12-16 months in used French oak barriques

pairings.why: Concentrated mineral Syrah and slow-cooked lamb are a classic Northern Rhone match.
The wine's mineral and dark fruit intensity matches gamey venison.
The wine's pepper and mineral character pairs well with artisan charcuterie.

history.summary: Clos de Cuminal is a small enclosed vineyard parcel that Pierre Gaillard identified as an exceptional site for old-vine Syrah in the Saint-Joseph appellation. The wine has been a signature of the domaine since the first vintage.

milestones.event: First vintage of Clos de Cuminal from the enclosed single-parcel vineyard

wines.description: Gaillard's single-parcel Saint-Joseph from the enclosed Clos de Cuminal vineyard. Old Syrah on granite delivering concentrated, mineral Northern Rhone character.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in old French oak foudres, no new oak

pairings.why: The wine's wild blueberry and violet aromas are the perfect match for roasted pigeon.

pairings.dish: Lamb chops with herbs and garlic
pairings.why: Classic Rhone pairing; elegant Syrah and herb-infused lamb.

pairings.dish: Duck breast with lentils du Puy
pairings.why: Earthy mineral lentils and duck fat meet the wine's silky fruit.

history.summary: The Gonon family has farmed Saint-Joseph vines since 1963. Brothers Pierre and Jean Gonon farm old Syrah vines on the granitic schist of Mauves with biodynamic-practicing methods, producing one of the appellation's most sought-after wines.

milestones.event: Gonon family begins cultivating Saint-Joseph parcels in Mauves
Pierre and Jean Gonon take over from their father; reputation grows internationally

wines.description: The Gonon Saint-Joseph is among the appellation's most elegant and sought-after wines. Old-vine Syrah on granite, no new oak, producing crystalline mineral purity.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Blanc Les Oliviers
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in old French oak foudres

pairings.why: The wine's almond and stone-fruit character mirrors the almond crust on turbot.
Rich Marsanne-based white suits creamy veal preparations.
The wine's waxy mineral character complements aged washed-rind cow's milk cheese.

history.summary: Les Oliviers is named for olive trees that grow in the Gonon vineyards above Mauves. The white Saint-Joseph from Marsanne and Roussanne is considered among the most serious whites produced in this appellation.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Oliviers blanc from the Marsanne and Roussanne parcels

wines.description: Gonon's benchmark white Saint-Joseph from Marsanne and Roussanne. Old foudre-vinified with waxy mineral complexity that develops beautifully over 10-15 years.

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in used French oak barriques and casks

pairings.why: Classic Northern Rhone pairing at its most accessible price point.
Everyday bistro fare finds the perfect companion in this honest Crozes.
Pork and smoked-olive Syrah are natural companions.

history.summary: Alain Graillot founded his domaine in 1985 after a career in industry, quickly establishing himself as one of Crozes-Hermitage's most respected producers. The estate rouge became a benchmark for the appellation.

milestones.event: Alain Graillot founds the domaine and produces his first vintage

wines.description: The classic Graillot Crozes-Hermitage, one of the Rhone Valley's most respected value wines. Honest, peppery Syrah with personality from the appellation's best ambassador.

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage La Guiraude
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak barriques, some new

pairings.why: The wine's concentration and mineral character match this classic Rhone pairing.
Rich beef stew with olives and herbs echoes the wine's olive and mineral notes.
The wine's iron and dark fruit complement gamey venison and earthy lentils.

history.summary: La Guiraude was identified by Graillot as the finest parcel on his estate, with old Syrah vines producing exceptional concentration and mineral depth. It was first bottled separately in 1990.

milestones.event: First separate bottling of La Guiraude from the old-vine single parcel

wines.description: Graillot's prestige single old-vine parcel Crozes-Hermitage. More concentrated and mineral than the appellation wine, approaching Hermitage quality at a significant price advantage.

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage Clos des Grives Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 16 months in used French oak barriques

taste.summary: The Clos des Grives is a biodynamically-farmed single parcel, producing one of the most elegant and mineral Crozes-Hermitage wines. Laurent Combier's biodynamic commitment produces exceptional purity.

pairings.dish: Duck magret with green peppercorn sauce
pairings.why: The wine's peppery mineral character aligns with the pepper sauce on duck.
Slow-roasted lamb with garlic suits the wine's dark fruit and herbal notes.
The wine's herbal notes and moderate tannins pair naturally with mushroom tart.

history.summary: Laurent Combier converted the Clos des Grives parcel to biodynamic farming and began separate bottling in 1990. It has become the standout wine of the Combier estate and a benchmark for biodynamic Crozes.

milestones.event: First separate bottling of Clos des Grives under biodynamic-practicing farming

wines.description: Combier's flagship biodynamic-farmed single-parcel Crozes-Hermitage. One of the most elegant and mineral wines in the appellation at an accessible price.

wines.name: Cornas Pur Noir
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in used French oak barriques and foudres

pairings.why: The wine's herb and dark fruit notes echo Provencale lamb stew.
Iron-mineral Cornas and well-aged beef are a natural pairing.
Game and earthy chestnuts meet the wine's mineral concentration.

history.summary: Stephane Robert founded Domaine du Tunnel in 1994 and produced his first Cornas in 1995. The name Pur Noir refers to the pure darkness of the granite-grown Syrah and the deeply colored wine it produces.

milestones.event: Stephane Robert founds Domaine du Tunnel in Saint-Peray
First vintage of Pur Noir Cornas

wines.description: Domaine du Tunnel's Cornas from granite terraces. Pure Syrah with iron-mineral density and smoked-herb character from a Saint-Peray specialist venturing into the adjacent Cornas appellation.

wines.name: Saint-Peray Marsanne
wines.oak_regime: 10 months in used French oak barrels

pairings.why: Marsanne's beeswax and almond notes are the classic match for white asparagus.
The wine's rich Marsanne texture is at home with cream-based poultry.
Mineral-rich white wine and saffron cream with monkfish is a classic combination.

history.summary: Saint-Peray's first vintage from Domaine du Tunnel in 1994. The domain has become the most visible producer of this small, underrated appellation adjacent to Cornas on the right bank of the Rhone.

milestones.event: First vintage of Saint-Peray Marsanne, establishing Tunnel as the appellation's reference

wines.description: The benchmark still Saint-Peray from the appellation's most consistent producer. Rich Marsanne with beeswax and mineral character at a very accessible price.

wines.name: Saint-Peray Brut Methode Traditionnelle
wines.oak_regime: Base wine in old barrels; second fermentation in bottle, 18-24 months on lees

pairings.why: The wine's mineral acidity and bubbles are excellent with oysters.
Almond and nutty notes match light pastry and cheese appetizers.
The wine's mineral freshness cuts through the richness of smoked fish.

history.summary: Saint-Peray sparkling is one of France's oldest traditional-method appellations, predating Champagne's commercial sparkling production according to some accounts. Domaine du Tunnel revives this tradition with their Brut.

milestones.event: First documented production of Saint-Peray sparkling wine using traditional method
Tunnel begins producing Saint-Peray Brut, reviving the appellation's sparkling tradition

wines.description: Domaine du Tunnel's traditional-method Saint-Peray sparkling, reviving the appellation's historic pre-Champagne sparkling tradition. Marsanne and Roussanne with almond and mineral character.

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage Les Pierrelles Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 14 months in used French oak barriques

pairings.why: The wine's herb and moderate fruit character are at home with roasted chicken.
Medium-weight Syrah and spiced lamb work well together.

pairings.dish: Pizza with Italian sausage and peppers
pairings.why: The wine's approachable fruit and herbs complement casual pizza.

history.summary: Domaine Belle was established in Larnage on the Crozes-Hermitage plateau and converted to organic farming early. Les Pierrelles (the little stones) refers to the pebbly soils of the plateau.

milestones.event: Domaine Belle founded in Larnage
First vintage of Les Pierrelles from the organic-farmed Larnage plateau parcels

wines.description: Domaine Belle's estate Crozes-Hermitage from organic Larnage parcels. Reliable, clean Syrah with peppery mineral character at an excellent everyday price.

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage Cuvee Louis Belle
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new and used French oak barriques

pairings.dish: Braised lamb shank with root vegetables
pairings.why: The wine's concentration and mineral depth suit slow-braised lamb.
Dark fruit in the wine mirrors the cherry reduction accompanying duck.
The firm tannins and mineral intensity need a well-marbled beef cut.

history.summary: The Cuvee Louis Belle is the prestige selection from Domaine Belle, named in honour of the founding member of the family. It is made only from the estate's finest parcels in exceptional vintages.

milestones.event: First vintage of Cuvee Louis Belle as the prestige selection from Domaine Belle

wines.description: Domaine Belle's prestige Crozes-Hermitage named for the estate founder. From the finest organic parcels with more concentration and mineral depth than Les Pierrelles.

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage Equinoxe
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in old French oak barriques, no new oak

pairings.why: The wine's mineral freshness and silky tannins pair surprisingly well with grilled salmon.
Blue and dark fruit in the wine mirrors cherry preparations with duck.

pairings.dish: Lentil and vegetable soup with smoked paprika
pairings.why: The wine's mineral quality and herbs elevate a rustic vegetable-based dish.

history.summary: Maxime Graillot founded his own Domaine des Lises in Mercurol in 2012, separate from his family's historic Alain Graillot domaine. Equinoxe is his flagship Crozes, vinified with precision and minimal intervention.

milestones.event: Maxime Graillot establishes Domaine des Lises in Mercurol; first vintage of Equinoxe

wines.description: Maxime Graillot's precision Crozes-Hermitage from Domaine des Lises. Precise mineral Syrah with zero new oak from a young producer showing the evolution of Northern Rhone winemaking.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in used French oak barriques

pairings.why: Accessible pepper-forward Syrah is the natural partner for grilled lamb sausage.
The wine's medium weight and pepper character work with pork and mushroom.
Simple, honest Syrah suits a charcuterie spread.

history.summary: The Faury family has farmed in Chavanay since 1979, producing both Condrieu and Saint-Joseph. The domaine is known for organic farming and a clean, precise house style.

milestones.event: Faury family establishes the domaine in Chavanay

wines.description: The Faury family's organic Saint-Joseph from Chavanay granite terraces. Clean, pepper-forward Syrah at an accessible price from a reliable family domaine.

wines.name: Condrieu
wines.oak_regime: 7 months in used French oak barrels

pairings.why: The wine's floral freshness pairs gently with spring asparagus.

pairings.dish: Grilled chicken with lemon and herbs

history.summary: The Faury family began producing Condrieu in 1985 from their Chavanay parcels. The wine represents an accessible entry into the appellation's floral Viognier character.

wines.description: The Faury family's organic Condrieu from Chavanay parcels. A lighter-touch entry into the appellation's floral Viognier style at an accessible price.

wines.name: Cote-Rotie La Landonne
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new and used French oak barriques

taste.summary: Delas's La Landonne from the Cote Brune, a powerful and mineral expression of the famous lieu-dit. The Delas version is known for its density and iron character typical of the schist-iron soils.

pairings.why: Iron-mineral Landonne needs a well-aged, marbled beef cut with fat.
Classic Rhone pairing with the wine's mineral character and dark olive notes.
Game intensity matches the wine's iron and dark fruit concentration.

history.summary: Delas Freres, one of the Rhone's historic negociant houses, produces La Landonne from the famous Cote Brune parcel. The wine was first produced in 1991 under the house's quality renovation.

milestones.event: Delas Freres first produces La Landonne as a separate single-vineyard bottling

wines.description: Delas's single-vineyard Cote-Rotie from the iron-rich Cote Brune La Landonne. Powerful, mineral Syrah with the ferrous intensity that defines this celebrated lieu-dit.

wines.name: Hermitage Domaine des Tourettes
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in new and used French oak barriques

taste.summary: Delas's flagship Hermitage, from parcels owned by the house on the granite hill. A reliable and well-structured expression of the appellation from one of the Northern Rhone's established negociant houses.

pairings.why: Classic Northern Rhone pairing; the wine's mineral structure and dark fruit match lamb perfectly.
Traditional French boiled beef requires a substantial, mineral red.
The wine's dark fruit and tobacco notes match game bird preparations.

history.summary: Delas Freres was founded in 1835 and has been producing Hermitage for generations. The Domaine des Tourettes label indicates estate-grown fruit from the house's own parcels on the Hermitage hill.

milestones.event: Delas Freres founded in the Northern Rhone
Domaine des Tourettes first produced as the estate Hermitage label

wines.description: Delas's flagship estate Hermitage from their own parcels on the granite hill. Reliable, well-structured Syrah with mineral depth from one of the Rhone's historic negociant houses.

wines.name: Hermitage Les Bessards
wines.oak_regime: 20 months in new French oak barriques

taste.summary: From the Les Bessards parcel, the most powerful and concentrated section of the Hermitage hill. Delas produces one of the appellation's most structured and age-worthy wines from this iconic site.

pairings.why: Massive mineral tannins require the most substantial beef preparation.
Game with strong flavours stands up to the wine's power and mineral intensity.
Firm sheep's-milk cheese softens the wine's iron tannins elegantly.

history.summary: Les Bessards is considered one of the two most powerful lieux-dits on the Hermitage hill. Delas has been producing a single-parcel Les Bessards since 1985 as their top Hermitage cuvee.

milestones.event: First separate bottling of Les Bessards as Delas's prestige Hermitage

wines.description: Delas's prestige Hermitage from the Les Bessards parcel, one of the hill's most powerful sites. Massively structured, iron-mineral Syrah demanding long cellaring.

wines.name: Cornas Chante-Perdrix
wines.oak_regime: 16 months in used French oak barriques

taste.summary: Chante-Perdrix (the singing partridge) is Delas's Cornas expression from granite terraces. One of the most accessible and reliable Cornas wines from a major negociant house.

pairings.why: The wine's name suggests the pairing; game bird and earthy lentils.
Classic Rhone preparation; the wine's black olive notes echo the dish.
Rich beef stew and firm tannins are a natural Northern Rhone combination.

history.summary: Chante-Perdrix has been Delas's Cornas since 1975. The evocative name (the singing partridge) reflects the wild game-bird landscapes of the granite Cornas hillsides.

milestones.event: First vintage of Chante-Perdrix as Delas's Cornas cuvee

wines.description: Delas's Cornas from granite terraces. Firm, iron-mineral Syrah with the smoking-olive character that defines this appellation, from a reliable negociant source.

wines.name: Saint-Joseph Les Granilites Blanc
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in used French oak barriques

taste.summary: Chapoutier's flagship white Saint-Joseph from biodynamic parcels of Marsanne and Roussanne. One of the finest expressions of these northern white varieties outside of Hermitage.

pairings.why: The wine's almond and mineral character aligns with roasted white fish and almond crust.

pairings.dish: Veal in cream sauce with morels
pairings.why: Rich Marsanne-based white suits cream-based veal with earthy mushrooms.
Hazelnut notes in the wine echo hazelnut butter on asparagus.

history.summary: Les Granilites Blanc is Chapoutier's flagship white Saint-Joseph, produced from biodynamic parcels planted with Marsanne and Roussanne on the granite terraces of the appellation's northern zone.

milestones.event: First vintage of Les Granilites Blanc from biodynamic Saint-Joseph parcels

wines.description: Chapoutier's biodynamic white Saint-Joseph, a Marsanne and Roussanne blend from granite parcels. Rich mineral white with hazelnut and peach character.

wines.name: Crozes-Hermitage La Petite Ruche Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in used French oak casks

taste.summary: Chapoutier's entry-level Crozes-Hermitage, widely available and biodynamically farmed. The most affordable way to access Chapoutier's Northern Rhone Syrah expertise.

pairings.dish: Grilled beef burger with grilled peppers
pairings.why: The wine's casual fruit and pepper character is ideal with a quality burger.
Moderate Crozes and olive-braised chicken are a reliable everyday pairing.
Classic French charcuterie table and accessible Northern Rhone Syrah.

history.summary: La Petite Ruche is Chapoutier's widely distributed entry-level Crozes-Hermitage, designed to be the most accessible wine in the house's Northern Rhone range. Biodynamically farmed across multiple parcels.

milestones.event: La Petite Ruche introduced as Chapoutier's entry-level Crozes-Hermitage

wines.description: Chapoutier's biodynamic entry-level Crozes-Hermitage. Accessible pepper-forward Syrah at the most affordable price point in the house's Northern Rhone range.

verified.address_quoted: 18 Avenue du Docteur Paul Durand, 26600 Tain-l'Hermitage

wines.name: Saint-Peray Les Tanneurs
wines.oak_regime: 10 months in used French oak barrels

taste.summary: Chapoutier's Saint-Peray from biodynamic Marsanne vines. One of the most accessible introductions to this underappreciated appellation; rich, textural and mineral.

pairings.why: Rich Marsanne and cream-based chicken is a classic regional pairing.

pairings.dish: Grilled sea bass with beurre blanc
pairings.why: The wine's mineral-rich texture and moderate acidity suit firm white fish.

pairings.dish: Roasted white asparagus with brown butter
pairings.why: Beeswax and almond notes in the wine are natural with asparagus and browned butter.

history.summary: Les Tanneurs is Chapoutier's Saint-Peray from biodynamic Marsanne parcels. The appellation is named after the tanneries that once occupied the hillsides above the town of Saint-Peray.

milestones.event: Chapoutier introduces Les Tanneurs as the house's Saint-Peray expression

wines.description: Chapoutier's biodynamic Saint-Peray from Marsanne vines. Rich, waxy mineral white introducing this underappreciated appellation adjacent to Cornas.
