milestones.event: First vintage bottled as a single-varietal Roussanne Vieilles Vignes

wines.description: 100% old-vine Roussanne from Beaucastel. Extraordinary hazelnut and truffle complexity.

wines.name: Chateau Rayas Chateauneuf-du-Pape Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Old large foudres, no new oak

taste.summary: 100% Grenache from sandy soils at low yield; deceptively light in colour but profound in aromatic complexity, with ethereal red fruit, silky texture, and great longevity.

pairings.dish: Roasted rack of lamb with thyme
pairings.why: High-acid Grenache cuts lamb fat while red fruit mirrors the herb-roasted notes.
Cherry fruit echoes the cherry and dried rose of old-vine Grenache.
Light-bodied but complex Grenache is a sommelier classic with roast pigeon.

history.summary: Founded by Louis Reynaud in 1923 on north-facing sandy soils at the highest elevation in Chateauneuf, the estate is now managed by Emmanuel and Frederic Reynaud. Rayas is the world's most famous 100% Grenache and commands cult prices despite modest yields and no name recognition outside specialist circles.

milestones.event: Louis Reynaud plants Grenache on sandy north-facing plateau in Chateauneuf
Jacques Reynaud takes over winemaking; establishes minimal-intervention style
Emmanuel Reynaud inherits the estate after Jacques Reynaud's death

wines.description: 100% old-vine Grenache from Chateau Rayas, one of the world's most coveted cult wines, made from sandy northern soils of Chateauneuf-du-Pape.

wines.name: Chateau Rayas Chateauneuf-du-Pape Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: 100% Grenache Blanc from sandy old vines; an unusual and complex white Chateauneuf with delicate florals, almond pastry texture, and remarkable longevity.

pairings.why: The almond and floral notes echo the dish's richness.
Nutty, waxy Grenache Blanc finds a partner in aged mountain cheese.
Medium body and subtle herb character complement white poultry.

history.summary: Made from 100% Grenache Blanc in the same low-intervention style as the red. Rayas blanc is extraordinarily rare with tiny production and commands prices approaching the red.

milestones.event: White cuvee first bottled under the Chateau Rayas label
Emmanuel Reynaud continues production after Jacques Reynaud's death

wines.description: 100% Grenache Blanc from Chateau Rayas, an exceptionally rare white Chateauneuf made in the same minimal-intervention style as the cult red.

wines.name: Pignan
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The second wine of Chateau Rayas from younger vines in the same sandy parcels; lighter-framed Grenache with the estate's signature aromatic finesse at an accessible price.

pairings.why: Fresh-framed Grenache with high acidity suits lighter lamb preparations.
Medium body and red fruit complement rabbit's mild gaminess.
Herbal and kirsch notes echo Provencal tapenade.

history.summary: The second label of Chateau Rayas from a separate parcel of younger vines adjacent to the Rayas vineyard. Pignan offers access to the Rayas style at a fraction of the price.

milestones.event: Pignan established as the second label of Chateau Rayas

wines.description: Second wine of Chateau Rayas from younger-vine Grenache, offering the estate's aromatic elegance at a more accessible price point.

wines.name: Domaine du Vieux Telegraphe La Crau Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large old foudres

taste.summary: The benchmark galets roules Chateauneuf from La Crau plateau: dark, rich Grenache-led blend with smoked meat, olive, and garrigue complexity on a full, mineral frame.

pairings.why: The wine's olive and garrigue notes mirror the slow-braised beef stew.
Grenache-led, full-bodied structure handles fat-rich lamb excellently.
Rich, smoked-meat aromatics are a natural match for confit.
Firm tannins cut through hard sheep's-milk cheese.

history.summary: The Brunier family has farmed the La Crau lieu-dit since 1898, one of the highest and stoniest plateaux in Chateauneuf-du-Pape. The galets roules (large rounded stones) retain heat and reflect it onto the vines, accelerating ripening. Vieux Telegraphe is named after the old Chappe telegraph tower on the hill.

milestones.event: Hippolyte Brunier plants vines on La Crau plateau
Estate grows significantly; La Crau wines bottled under the Vieux Telegraphe name
Daniel and Frederic Brunier take over; international recognition follows

wines.description: Flagship Chateauneuf-du-Pape from the Brunier family's La Crau plateau, named for the ancient Chappe telegraph tower on the hillside.

wines.name: Domaine du Vieux Telegraphe La Crau Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Old demi-muids and foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: A full-bodied white Chateauneuf from the same La Crau galets plateau; aromatic, generous, and approachable, with Grenache Blanc's stone-fruit depth and good freshness.

pairings.why: White Chateauneuf's full body and herb notes complement Mediterranean fish.
Anise and stone-fruit aromas find echoes in Provencal fish stew.
The wine's garrigue notes pair naturally with olive-based Provencal snacks.

history.summary: The white Vieux Telegraphe is produced from old Grenache Blanc, Clairette, Roussanne, and Bourboulenc vines on the La Crau plateau. A multi-variety blend fermented and aged in old wood, it reflects the stony terroir in a generous, herb-accented style.

milestones.event: White cuvee first produced under the La Crau label

wines.description: White Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Vieux Telegraphe's La Crau plateau, blending Grenache Blanc, Clairette, Roussanne, and Bourboulenc.

wines.name: Telegramme Chateauneuf-du-Pape
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The accessible second label of Vieux Telegraphe; a generously fruited, herb-accented Chateauneuf for early drinking with the estate's characteristic garrigue signature.

pairings.why: Spiced lamb sausage meets the wine's warm spice and red fruit.

pairings.dish: Grilled chicken with herbs de Provence
pairings.why: Medium-bodied Grenache at its most food-friendly with herb-roasted chicken.
The wine's garrigue notes echo the Provencal vegetable stew.

history.summary: Telegramme was launched in 2000 as the accessible second wine of the Brunier family's Vieux Telegraphe estate. It draws on younger-vine parcels in Chateauneuf and is designed for early drinking rather than extended cellaring.

milestones.event: Telegramme launched as the second label of Vieux Telegraphe

wines.description: The approachable second label of Vieux Telegraphe; a herb-accented, fruit-forward Chateauneuf-du-Pape for early enjoyment.

wines.name: Clos des Papes Chateauneuf-du-Pape Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in old foudres and demi-muids

taste.summary: One of the most consistently praised Chateauneufs, the Paul Avril estate produces dense, structured red Chateauneuf with Grenache-led depth, Mourvedre spine, and superb ageing potential.

pairings.why: Classic Chateauneuf-lamb pairing; full-bodied Grenache cuts through the richness.
Earthy iron notes complement wild fungal flavours.
Dense structure and dark fruit handle rich grilled beef.

history.summary: The Avril family has been making wine at Clos des Papes since at least 1896, farming some of the oldest vines in the appellation. Paul Avril took over in the 1970s and built the estate into one of Chateauneuf's most admired domaines. Winemaker Vincent Avril continues the traditional foudre-based approach.

milestones.event: Earliest documented production at Clos des Papes under the Avril family
Paul Avril takes over and focuses the domaine on quality
Vincent Avril joins as winemaker

wines.description: Flagship red Chateauneuf-du-Pape from the Avril family estate, one of the appellation's most consistent and structured expressions.

wines.name: Clos des Papes Chateauneuf-du-Pape Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: A broad, aromatic white Chateauneuf blending Grenache Blanc, Roussanne, Clairette, and Bourboulenc; generous stone fruit, Provencal herbs, and a clean mineral finish.

pairings.why: White Chateauneuf's full body and herb character match the dish's complexity.
Anise and stone fruit complement asparagus tart beautifully.
The wine's creaminess and weight pair well with cream sauce.

history.summary: The white Clos des Papes is a multi-variety blend fermented in old foudres; production is tiny compared to the red. The Avril family's vineyards include old parcels of Grenache Blanc and Clairette that add characteristic Provencal herb notes.

milestones.event: White cuvee first produced by the Avril family at Clos des Papes

wines.description: White Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Clos des Papes, a full-bodied blend of Grenache Blanc, Roussanne, Clairette, and Bourboulenc.

wines.name: Domaine du Pegau Cuvee Reservee
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in old foudres

taste.summary: A powerful, traditional-style Chateauneuf with deep Grenache-driven fruit, garrigue perfume, leather, and a generous, warm, structured finish.

pairings.why: Structured Grenache with warm spice is the classic match for Provencal lamb.
Dense fruit and leather notes complement game stew.

pairings.dish: Grilled beef entrecote with bone marrow
pairings.why: Warm, concentrated Grenache handles rich beef and marrow.

history.summary: Paul and Laurence Feraud founded Pegau in 1987, farming old-vine Grenache in the traditional Chateauneuf manner. The Cuvee Reservee is the estate flagship, aged in old foudres with no filtration, in the style of the appellation's great traditional estates.

milestones.event: Domaine du Pegau established by Paul and Laurence Feraud
Cuvee Reservee earns recognition from international critics

wines.description: Flagship Grenache-dominant Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Domaine du Pegau, a traditional-style powerhouse aged in old foudres.

wines.name: Domaine du Pegau Cuvee da Capo
wines.oak_regime: Old large foudres; extended elevage

taste.summary: Made only in exceptional years from the oldest Grenache vines at Pegau; an extreme, deeply concentrated Chateauneuf of extraordinary power and longevity.

pairings.why: The wine's massive concentration and warmth demands equally rich food.
Firm tannins and concentration handle the intensity of hard aged cheese.

history.summary: Da Capo (Italian for 'from the beginning') is Pegau's exceptional-vintage-only prestige cuvee from the oldest Grenache vines. ABV regularly exceeds 15.5%, making it one of the most powerful wines in France. Production is tiny and the wine is highly sought after by collectors.

milestones.event: First vintage of Cuvee da Capo produced at Domaine du Pegau
Second release; the 2003 heat vintage produces a legendary da Capo

wines.description: Exceptional-vintage prestige cuvee from Domaine du Pegau, 100% Grenache of extreme concentration and power from the oldest vines.

wines.name: Domaine du Pegau Cuvee Laurence
wines.oak_regime: Extended in old large foudres, longer than Reservee

taste.summary: Between the Reservee and da Capo in style; rich, deep, and complex with southern warmth and Mourvedre backbone adding iron and herb notes.

pairings.why: Deep fruit and iron notes handle the char and fat of prime beef.
The wine's herb and truffle-adjacent earth notes pair beautifully.

history.summary: Cuvee Laurence was created as an intermediate prestige cuvee at Pegau, named for Laurence Feraud. It sits between the Reservee and the rare da Capo in terms of concentration and extraction.

milestones.event: First vintage of Cuvee Laurence at Domaine du Pegau

wines.description: Prestige cuvee from Domaine du Pegau, a deeply concentrated Chateauneuf between the Reservee and the exceptional-vintage da Capo.

wines.name: Domaine de la Janasse Chateauneuf-du-Pape Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: 20 months in large old foudres

taste.summary: 100% old-vine Grenache of great depth and complexity from Janasse's most celebrated parcels; perfumed, rich, and structured for long ageing.

pairings.why: Classic pairing; old-vine Grenache power matches slow-roasted lamb.
Concentrated cherry fruit echoes in the wine's morello character.
Full-bodied Grenache and hard aged cheese are natural partners.

history.summary: Domaine de la Janasse is a family estate in Courthezone, farmed by the Sabon family. The Vieilles Vignes cuvee draws from the oldest Grenache parcels in the appellation, with vines averaging over 60 years of age, producing tiny berries of intense concentration.

milestones.event: First vintage of Vieilles Vignes cuvee at La Janasse
Christophe Sabon establishes the estate's international reputation

wines.description: 100% old-vine Grenache prestige cuvee from Domaine de la Janasse, one of the most admired expressions of old Chateauneuf Grenache.

wines.name: Domaine de la Janasse Chaupin
wines.oak_regime: Old large foudres

taste.summary: Chaupin is a single-lieu-dit Grenache from sandy soils in the north of Chateauneuf; exceptional richness and concentration with silky but persistent tannins.

pairings.why: Dense concentrated Grenache matches bold game flavours.
Dark fruit and earthy minerality pair naturally with Provencal braised beef.

history.summary: Chaupin is a single-parcel cuvee from the Chaupin lieu-dit in the north of Chateauneuf-du-Pape, where sandy soils produce particularly aromatic and silky Grenache. First produced in the same year as Vieilles Vignes.

milestones.event: First vintage of Chaupin cuvee from the lieu-dit of the same name

wines.description: Single-parcel old-vine Grenache from the Chaupin lieu-dit of Chateauneuf-du-Pape, a concentrated and silky prestige cuvee from La Janasse.

wines.name: Domaine de la Janasse Chateauneuf-du-Pape Classique
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; not as long as prestige cuvees

taste.summary: The approachable entry-level Chateauneuf from La Janasse; fruit-forward, herb-accented, and generously structured, ideal for drinking in its first decade.

pairings.dish: Grilled lamb chops with herbes de Provence
pairings.why: Classic herb-forward Grenache is the ideal partner for simple grilled lamb.
Spiced lamb finds a willing companion in fruit-forward Grenache.

pairings.dish: Provencal pizza with olives and anchovies
pairings.why: Garrigue notes and lush fruit echo the Provencal toppings.

history.summary: The Janasse Classique is the estate's entry Chateauneuf, blending fruit from younger vines across the estate into an approachable, herb-accented expression of the appellation.

milestones.event: La Janasse Classique established as the estate's everyday Chateauneuf

wines.description: Entry-level Chateauneuf-du-Pape from La Janasse, herb-forward and fruit-driven, for early enjoyment.

wines.name: Domaine Charvin Chateauneuf-du-Pape
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak; minimal sulphur

taste.summary: A more elegant, mineral-driven Chateauneuf with high-toned red fruit, precision, and purity. Charvin's approach favours natural winemaking with minimal intervention.

pairings.why: Light-to-medium-bodied Grenache elegance is a sommelier classic with pigeon.
Red fruit and floral notes complement duck without overwhelming it.
Lavender notes in the wine find an echo in the Provencal herb crust.

history.summary: Laurent Charvin took over from his father in 1991, converting to organic farming and minimal-intervention winemaking. The domaine farms sandy and galets soils in the lieu-dit Orange road of Chateauneuf, producing an unusually elegant and precise expression of Grenache.

milestones.event: Laurent Charvin takes over the domaine and converts to organic farming
International critics begin to recognise Charvin as a distinct, elegant house style

wines.description: An elegant, mineral-driven Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Laurent Charvin, known for precision and purity rather than power.

wines.name: Clos Saint-Jean Deus-Ex Machina
wines.oak_regime: 30% new French oak barrique, remainder old foudres

taste.summary: An extraordinarily powerful, densely concentrated Chateauneuf with coffee, dark fruit, and iron; a long-ageing prestige cuvee made in exceptional years only.

pairings.why: Massive structure demands equally intense food; truffle and game are natural.

pairings.dish: Braised oxtail with roasted root vegetables
pairings.why: Deep dark fruit and earthy iron match long-braised beef.

history.summary: Clos Saint-Jean is a family estate run by Pascal and Vincent Maurel. Deus-Ex Machina was created in 2003 as an exceptional-vintage prestige cuvee from the estate's oldest Grenache vines, blended with Mourvedre and Syrah for additional structure and ageing potential.

milestones.event: First vintage of Deus-Ex Machina; a response to the legendary 2003 heat vintage

wines.description: Exceptional-vintage prestige cuvee from Clos Saint-Jean, an ultra-concentrated Chateauneuf-du-Pape made from old Grenache vines.

wines.name: Clos Saint-Jean La Combe des Fous
wines.oak_regime: Old large foudres; extended maceration

taste.summary: Structured, dark, and inky Chateauneuf with Mourvedre backbone adding iron and tobacco notes alongside Grenache's dark fruit and spice.

pairings.dish: Rack of lamb with black truffle crust
pairings.why: Mourvedre backbone and herb notes are classic with lamb and truffle.
Concentrated dark fruit and firm tannins handle char-grilled beef.

history.summary: La Combe des Fous is the intermediate prestige cuvee at Clos Saint-Jean, sitting between the estate classique and the extreme Deus-Ex Machina. It draws from older-vine parcels with a higher proportion of Mourvedre than the classic.

milestones.event: La Combe des Fous first produced as a distinct cuvee at Clos Saint-Jean

wines.description: Prestige cuvee from Clos Saint-Jean, a Mourvedre-accented Chateauneuf with dark inky fruit and tobacco complexity.

wines.name: Clos Saint-Jean Chateauneuf-du-Pape
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres

taste.summary: The estate Chateauneuf from Clos Saint-Jean; approachable, fruit-forward, and herb-accented with the estate's characteristic dark-fruit personality.

pairings.why: Classic Grenache-lamb match; herb notes echo the stew's aromatics.
Pork sausage finds a companion in the wine's ripe, warm fruit.

history.summary: The estate wine of Clos Saint-Jean, a family property run by the Maurel brothers. It draws on fruit from across the domaine's parcels to produce an approachable, early-drinking Chateauneuf.

milestones.event: Clos Saint-Jean estate Chateauneuf established by the Maurel family

wines.description: Estate Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Clos Saint-Jean; fruit-forward and herb-accented, approachable in its first decade.

wines.name: Domaine de Marcoux Chateauneuf-du-Pape Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: Old large foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: A biodynamic Grenache of great purity; seductive, perfumed, and silky with exotic spice and concentrated fruit on a long, refined mineral finish.

pairings.dish: Leg of lamb slow-roasted with saffron and spice
pairings.why: Exotic spice notes in the wine echo the aromatic North-African-inflected lamb.
Silky tannins and concentrated cherry fruit complement duck breast.

history.summary: Domaine de Marcoux is one of Chateauneuf's earliest and most committed biodynamic estates, converted by Sophie and Catherine Armenier. The Vieilles Vignes cuvee from 80+ year-old Grenache vines is the estate's most celebrated wine.

milestones.event: Vieilles Vignes cuvee first produced from the oldest Grenache at Marcoux
Full biodynamic conversion completed at Domaine de Marcoux

wines.description: 100% old-vine Grenache prestige cuvee from biodynamic Domaine de Marcoux; seductive, perfumed, and silky.

wines.name: Domaine de Marcoux Chateauneuf-du-Pape
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The estate classique Chateauneuf from biodynamic Marcoux; aromatic, silky, and complex with great purity of fruit and the estate's characteristic Provencal herb character.

pairings.why: Medium-bodied biodynamic Grenache matches herb-roasted chicken beautifully.

pairings.dish: Roasted lamb with olives and tomatoes
pairings.why: Provencal herb and olive notes echo in the wine.

history.summary: The estate wine of biodynamic Domaine de Marcoux from Grenache, Mourvedre, and Syrah across the domaine's parcels. Farmed without synthetic inputs since the early 1990s.

milestones.event: Domaine de Marcoux classique Chateauneuf first produced

wines.description: Estate Chateauneuf-du-Pape from biodynamic Domaine de Marcoux; aromatic and silky, with great Grenache purity.

wines.name: Le Vieux Donjon Chateauneuf-du-Pape
wines.oak_regime: Old large foudres; no new oak; no filtration

taste.summary: Traditional-style Chateauneuf from the Michel family: deeply coloured, earthy, herb-driven, and structured with good acidity and firm tannins built for ageing.

pairings.why: Earthy and herb-driven, the wine mirrors the slow-braised beef and olive dish.

pairings.dish: Roasted rack of lamb with thyme and rosemary
pairings.why: Herb notes and firm tannins make this a natural with roasted lamb.
Iron and leather notes match the gaminess of roasted pigeon.

history.summary: The Michel family estate in Chateauneuf-du-Pape, run with a conservative, traditional philosophy. The domaine produces a single Chateauneuf rouge from old Grenache and Syrah vines, unfiltered and aged in old foudres.

milestones.event: Le Vieux Donjon established as an estate-bottled Chateauneuf
Second generation Michel family continues traditional winemaking approach

wines.description: Traditional-style Chateauneuf-du-Pape from the Michel family; earthy, herb-driven, unfiltered, and built for ageing.

wines.name: Domaine de la Mordoree La Reine des Bois Chateauneuf-du-Pape
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in old foudres and demi-muids

taste.summary: The prestige Chateauneuf from La Mordoree; dark, rich, and muscular with Mourvedre backbone and a silky tannic finish. Consistently one of the appellation's finest.

pairings.dish: Grilled duck breast with fig compote
pairings.why: Dark fruit and Mourvedre depth handle duck's richness and the fig's sweetness.

pairings.dish: Roasted leg of lamb with thyme
pairings.why: Classic Grenache-lamb pairing; herb notes echo the Provencal seasoning.
Inky fruit and tobacco notes complement gamey slow-cooked boar.

history.summary: La Mordoree was founded in the 1980s in Tavel and expanded into Chateauneuf-du-Pape. The Reine des Bois ('Queen of the Woods') is the estate's flagship Chateauneuf, blending old Grenache with Mourvedre for depth and ageing potential.

milestones.event: Reine des Bois first produced as a distinct Chateauneuf cuvee
La Mordoree's Chateauneuf recognised among the appellation's top estates

wines.description: Flagship Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Domaine de la Mordoree; dark, rich, and Mourvedre-accented with excellent ageing potential.

wines.name: Domaine de la Mordoree La Plume du Peintre Chateauneuf-du-Pape
wines.oak_regime: Old demi-muids and foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: White Chateauneuf from La Mordoree; aromatic and generously textured, with white peach, honey, and herb notes from a multi-variety southern blend.

pairings.why: Full-bodied white Chateauneuf is the classic Provencal accompaniment to fish stew.
The wine's weight handles shellfish richness while acidity cuts through butter.
Anise and herb notes complement artichoke; full body handles the aioli.

history.summary: La Plume du Peintre ('the Painter's Quill') is the white Chateauneuf of La Mordoree, first produced in 2003 from old Grenache Blanc, Roussanne, and Clairette vines in the estate's holdings.

milestones.event: La Plume du Peintre first produced as a white Chateauneuf

wines.description: White Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Domaine de la Mordoree; aromatic, generously textured, and expressive of the southern white varieties.

wines.name: Chateau La Nerthe Chateauneuf-du-Pape Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in old large foudres

taste.summary: The estate red from one of Chateauneuf's most ancient properties; balanced and approachable with dark fruit, garrigue, and a structured tannic framework.

pairings.dish: Roasted lamb shoulder with herbes de Provence
pairings.why: Dark fruit and garrigue notes find a natural companion in herb-roasted lamb.
Full body and firm tannins handle charred beef well.
The wine's tannic structure pairs naturally with aged hard cheeses.

history.summary: Chateau La Nerthe is one of the oldest properties in Chateauneuf-du-Pape, with documented viticulture dating to at least the 16th century. Acquired by the Richard family (via Frey et al.) in 1985, the estate has been extensively renovated and now produces across several cuvees from 90 hectares.

milestones.event: First documented wine production at Chateau La Nerthe
David Brunier and Richard family partnership acquires and renovates the chateau

wines.description: Estate red Chateauneuf-du-Pape from one of the appellation's oldest properties, Chateau La Nerthe; balanced and approachable with dark fruit and garrigue.

wines.name: Chateau La Nerthe Chateauneuf-du-Pape Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres

taste.summary: A fresh, aromatic multi-variety white Chateauneuf from La Nerthe; lighter-textured than the Beaucastel or Pegau whites, with floral perfume and clean acidity.

pairings.why: Floral, herb-accented white Chateauneuf pairs naturally with vegetable dishes.

pairings.dish: Sea bass en papillote with lemon
pairings.why: Fresh acidity and citrus notes complement delicate fish.

history.summary: The white La Nerthe is a blend of all four primary white varieties grown at the estate. After the 1985 renovation, the estate began producing a modern, aromatic white Chateauneuf in old foudres.

milestones.event: White La Nerthe first produced after renovation of the estate

wines.description: Fresh, aromatic multi-variety white Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Chateau La Nerthe.

wines.name: Chateau La Nerthe Clos de Beauvenir Blanc
wines.oak_regime: New and old French oak barrique

taste.summary: La Nerthe's prestige white Chateauneuf from a walled Roussanne enclosure; waxy, complex, and age-worthy with beeswax, hazelnut, and truffle character.

pairings.why: Beeswax and truffle notes in the wine echo the earthy mushroom richness.
Full-bodied, waxy Roussanne matches the richness of langoustine.

history.summary: Clos de Beauvenir is produced from a walled enclosure of old Roussanne vines at La Nerthe, given barrel fermentation and ageing for extra complexity. It is the prestige white and the estate's most direct competition with Beaucastel's celebrated white Chateauneuf.

milestones.event: Clos de Beauvenir first produced as a distinct prestige white from a walled Roussanne enclosure

wines.description: Prestige white Chateauneuf-du-Pape from a walled Roussanne enclosure at Chateau La Nerthe; waxy, complex, and age-worthy.

wines.name: Chateau La Nerthe Cuvee des Cadettes Rouge
wines.oak_regime: 20 months in French oak barriques, one-third new

taste.summary: The prestige red Chateauneuf of La Nerthe; Mourvedre-accented, deeply inky, and firmly structured, requiring time in bottle to reveal its complexity.

pairings.why: Powerful tannins and iron-herb notes demand slow-braised lamb.
Mourvedre's iron and herb depth are natural with rich game and truffle.

history.summary: Cuvee des Cadettes is the prestige red of Chateau La Nerthe, first produced in 1990 from old Grenache and Mourvedre parcels. The use of new oak barriques distinguishes it from the traditional foudre-aged estate red.

milestones.event: Cuvee des Cadettes first produced as the prestige red at La Nerthe

wines.description: Prestige red Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Chateau La Nerthe, Mourvedre-accented and firmly structured for long ageing.

wines.name: Tardieu-Laurent Chateauneuf-du-Pape Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: 16 months in small French oak barrique, one-third new

taste.summary: Negociant Tardieu-Laurent sources old-vine Grenache and Mourvedre from Chateauneuf; the result is a powerful, oak-influenced, concentrated cuvee for extended ageing.

pairings.why: Rich oak and concentration demand equally rich food.
Dark fruit and coffee notes complement the richness of duck confit.

history.summary: Michel Tardieu and Dominique Laurent created this Chateauneuf cuvee sourced from old-vine parcels in the appellation. The negociant approach allows selection of the best fruit each vintage. Oak-influenced in style, it differs from the traditional foudre-aged approach of most Chateauneuf estates.

milestones.event: Tardieu-Laurent Chateauneuf Vieilles Vignes first produced by the negociant partnership

wines.description: Negociant prestige Chateauneuf-du-Pape from Tardieu-Laurent; old-vine Grenache-led, barrel-influenced, and concentrated.

wines.name: Famille Perrin Les Sinards Chateauneuf-du-Pape
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The Perrin family's approachable Chateauneuf from the Les Sinards lieu-dit; expressive, herb-accented, and fruit-forward in the characteristic Perrin Grenache-driven style.

pairings.why: Herb and fruit-forward Grenache matches roast chicken with olive paste.
Classic Grenache-lamb match; accessible and food-friendly.

history.summary: Les Sinards is the Perrin family's Chateauneuf-du-Pape sourced from the Les Sinards lieu-dit adjacent to the Beaucastel estate. Produced in larger volume than Beaucastel, it offers access to the Perrin style at a more accessible price.

milestones.event: Les Sinards first produced as a separate Chateauneuf label by the Perrin family

wines.description: Famille Perrin's approachable Chateauneuf-du-Pape from the Les Sinards lieu-dit; herb-accented and fruit-forward.

wines.name: Domaine Santa Duc Gigondas Prestige des Hautes Garrigues
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in old large foudres

taste.summary: A structured, mineral Gigondas from high-altitude garrigue parcels; more austere and herb-driven than fruit-forward, with excellent ageing potential.

pairings.dish: Grilled lamb ribs with herbes de Provence
pairings.why: Mountain herb notes find a natural match with herb-crusted lamb.
Firm structure and iron notes complement gamey wild boar.

history.summary: Domaine Santa Duc has farmed the Gigondas hillsides since 1874. The Prestige des Hautes Garrigues cuvee draws from the highest altitude parcels in the appellation, where garrigue and mineral notes dominate. Yves Gras runs the estate today.

milestones.event: Prestige des Hautes Garrigues established as a distinct high-altitude cuvee

wines.description: Prestige Gigondas from high-altitude garrigue parcels at Domaine Santa Duc; structured, mineral, and built for ageing.

wines.name: Domaine Santa Duc Gigondas
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The approachable Gigondas from Santa Duc; generous, herb-accented, and fruit-forward with the estate's characteristic mineral freshness.

pairings.why: Classic Grenache-lamb pairing at its most uncomplicated and satisfying.
Medium-bodied Gigondas handles spiced sausage and Provencal vegetables well.

history.summary: The estate Gigondas of Santa Duc, sourced from old Grenache vines across the domaine's various parcels in the Gigondas appellation. An approachable, herb-accented introduction to the estate's style.

milestones.event: Santa Duc classique Gigondas established

wines.description: Estate Gigondas from Domaine Santa Duc; herb-accented, fruit-forward, and approachable.

wines.name: Domaine Raspail-Ay Gigondas
wines.oak_regime: Old large foudres; no new oak; no filtration

taste.summary: Traditional Gigondas from the Ay family; old-vine Grenache unfiltered and aged in old foudres, offering excellent value and classic garrigue-driven Southern Rhone personality.

pairings.why: Classic Grenache-based Gigondas with traditional slow-braised lamb.
Firm tannins and earthy character pair well with aged sheep's-milk cheese.
Iron and earthy mineral notes complement wild mushrooms.

history.summary: The Ay family has farmed Gigondas for several generations. Dominique Ay runs the domaine today in a traditional style: no filtration, no new oak, old foudres only. The wine is one of the most consistent and honest expressions of the Gigondas appellation.

milestones.event: Raspail-Ay established as an estate-bottled Gigondas
Dominique Ay takes over; maintains traditional no-filtration approach

wines.description: Traditional Gigondas from Domaine Raspail-Ay; old-vine Grenache, unfiltered, and aged in old foudres. Honest, classically styled, and excellent value.

wines.name: Chateau de Saint Cosme Gigondas
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres and demi-muids; no new oak

taste.summary: Estate Gigondas from Louis Barruol's historic property; structured, mineral, and herb-driven with great food-compatibility and moderate ageing potential.

pairings.dish: Grilled lamb cutlets with herbes de Provence
pairings.why: Classic Grenache-herb match with straightforward grilled lamb.
Full-bodied Gigondas handles burgers with Provencal condiments.
Earthy mineral notes complement wild mushroom dishes.

history.summary: Chateau de Saint Cosme is one of the oldest continuous wine estates in Gigondas, with a history dating to Gallo-Roman times. Louis Barruol took over in 1995 and revived the estate's reputation. The estate Gigondas draws on old vines planted on the village's historic terraces.

milestones.event: Louis Barruol takes over Chateau de Saint Cosme
First vintage of the revived estate Gigondas under Louis Barruol

wines.description: Estate Gigondas from the historic Chateau de Saint Cosme; structured, mineral, and herb-driven.

wines.name: Chateau de Saint Cosme Hominis Fides
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak; minimal sulphur

taste.summary: The prestige single-parcel Gigondas from Saint Cosme; 100% old-vine Grenache of extraordinary concentration and mineral depth, among the finest wines of the appellation.

pairings.why: Concentrated old-vine Grenache holds its own against truffle and prime lamb.
High-acid, mineral Grenache complements gamey venison beautifully.

history.summary: Hominis Fides is the prestige single-parcel cuvee of Chateau de Saint Cosme, from the oldest Grenache vines on the estate's galets soils. First produced in 2005, it is made only in exceptional years and is the pinnacle of Barruol's Gigondas production.

milestones.event: First vintage of Hominis Fides from old Grenache at Saint Cosme

wines.description: Prestige single-parcel old-vine Grenache Gigondas from Chateau de Saint Cosme; concentrated, mineral, and one of the appellation's finest.

wines.name: Chateau de Saint Cosme Le Claux
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres and used demi-muids

taste.summary: A Mourvedre-accented Gigondas from the Le Claux parcel; more structured and austere than the estate Gigondas, with iron and wild herb character demanding several years of age.

pairings.why: Mourvedre's iron notes are a perfect foil for gamey wild boar.
Firm tannins and mineral character match hard aged cheese.

history.summary: Le Claux is a single-parcel Gigondas cuvee from Saint Cosme, drawn from a high-altitude clay-limestone parcel with more Mourvedre than the estate blend. First produced in 2002 as Louis Barruol explored single-terroir expressions.

milestones.event: Le Claux first produced as a single-parcel cuvee at Saint Cosme

wines.description: Single-parcel Mourvedre-accented Gigondas from Chateau de Saint Cosme; structured, austere, and built for ageing.

wines.name: Chateau de Saint Cosme Le Poste
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres only; no new oak

taste.summary: A third single-parcel Gigondas from Saint Cosme, somewhat lighter and more floral than Le Claux; Cinsault adds a fresh, aromatic quality to the Grenache core.

pairings.why: Floral Grenache with Cinsault freshness pairs well with lighter pork preparations.
Lighter-framed Gigondas with aromatic freshness suits grilled vegetables.

history.summary: Le Poste is a single-parcel Gigondas from Saint Cosme, from a parcel near the old postal road through the village. It includes Cinsault for freshness, making it the most approachable of the Saint Cosme single-parcel Gigondas range.

milestones.event: Le Poste first produced as a single-parcel cuvee at Saint Cosme

wines.description: Single-parcel Gigondas from Chateau de Saint Cosme, blending Grenache and Cinsault for freshness and floral charm.

wines.name: Domaine du Cayron Gigondas
wines.oak_regime: Old large foudres only; no new oak; very traditional

taste.summary: Old-school, artisanal Gigondas from the Faraud family; pure Grenache-led fruit, excellent value, and uncompromising old-foudre tradition. A perennial insider favourite.

pairings.dish: Grilled lamb kebabs with Provencal herbs
pairings.why: Classic herb-and-cherry Grenache character matches grilled lamb.
Traditional Gigondas with traditional Provencal mutton stew.
Kirsch and mineral freshness pair well with olive tapenade.

history.summary: The Faraud family has made Gigondas the traditional way for decades; old foudres, no new oak, no filtration, and long maceration. Domaine du Cayron is one of the most honest and underrated estates in the appellation.

milestones.event: Domaine du Cayron estate wine first bottled by the Faraud family

wines.description: Traditional old-foudre Gigondas from the Faraud family; pure Grenache fruit, excellent value, and an insider favourite.

wines.name: Tardieu-Laurent Gigondas
wines.oak_regime: 14 months in used French oak barrique

taste.summary: A negociant Gigondas in the Tardieu-Laurent house style; sourced from select old-vine parcels, oak-influenced and concentrated.

pairings.why: Concentrated, oaky Gigondas matches well with rich grilled beef.
Dark fruit and spice complement mushroom and cream pasta.

history.summary: Tardieu-Laurent's Gigondas is sourced from old-vine parcels by negociant Michel Tardieu. It follows the house style of barrel ageing and selection, producing a concentrated, structured Gigondas.

milestones.event: First Gigondas produced by Tardieu-Laurent negociant

wines.description: Negociant Gigondas from Tardieu-Laurent; concentrated, barrel-aged, and structured from old-vine Southern Rhone fruit.

wines.name: Le Sang des Cailloux Cuvee Doucinello
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel and old foudres

taste.summary: A dry, mineral Vacqueyras rose from Grenache and Cinsault; vibrant red fruit and fresh herbal notes with a clean, dry finish. Ideal for warm southern evenings.

pairings.dish: Grilled sardines with lemon and herbs
pairings.why: High-acid dry rose cuts through oily fish perfectly.
Herb-accented dry rose is the Provencal summer companion.
Crisp acidity and red fruit contrast nicely with tangy goat cheese.

history.summary: Sang des Cailloux is the reference estate for Vacqueyras AOC. The Doucinello is their rose cuvee, a dry, mineral expression of Grenache and Cinsault typical of the appellation's summer drinking style.

milestones.event: Cuvee Doucinello established as the rose of Sang des Cailloux

wines.description: Dry mineral Vacqueyras rose from Le Sang des Cailloux, blending Grenache and Cinsault for vibrant fruit and freshness.

wines.name: Le Sang des Cailloux Cuvee Lopy
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres and demi-muids; no new oak

taste.summary: The flagship red Vacqueyras from Sang des Cailloux; structured, mineral, and herb-driven, with excellent depth and length for the appellation.

pairings.why: Classic Southern Rhone pairing; herb notes and firm tannins handle lamb.
Mineral iron and dark fruit complement duck and olive.
Firm mineral tannins match the intensity of aged goat cheese.

history.summary: Cuvee Lopy is the flagship red of Sang des Cailloux, the estate that arguably defines what Vacqueyras should taste like. Serge Ferigoule runs the domaine with minimal intervention, old foudres, and no new oak.

milestones.event: Cuvee Lopy first produced as the flagship red at Sang des Cailloux

wines.description: Flagship red Vacqueyras from Le Sang des Cailloux; structured, mineral, and herb-driven with excellent depth.

wines.name: Domaine Brusset Vacqueyras
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: Approachable, fruit-forward Vacqueyras from the Brusset family; good value, herb-accented, and excellent for weeknight drinking.

pairings.dish: Grilled pork ribs with herbes de Provence
pairings.why: Medium-bodied Grenache matches grilled pork beautifully.
Herb notes and fresh cherry fruit pair naturally with Niçoise flavours.

history.summary: Domaine Brusset is a family estate in Cairanne with vineyards across several Southern Rhone appellations including Vacqueyras. Their Vacqueyras is produced from Grenache-dominant plots in the appellation.

milestones.event: Brusset Vacqueyras first produced from estate parcels in the appellation

wines.description: Approachable, fruit-forward Vacqueyras from Domaine Brusset; herb-accented and excellent value.

wines.name: Domaine La Soumade Rasteau Cuvee Confiance
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres and used barriques

taste.summary: A structured, dark Rasteau from the Romero family estate; Grenache dominant with iron, cocoa, and Provencal herb notes and a firm mineral finish.

pairings.why: Structured Rasteau handles slow-roasted lamb with ease.
Dark fruit and cocoa notes pair well with aged cheese and honey.

history.summary: Domaine La Soumade is one of the reference estates of the Rasteau AOC, located in the village of Rasteau in the northern Vaucluse. Andre Romero and his family have built the estate's reputation on structured, age-worthy Grenache.

milestones.event: Cuvee Confiance established as the top red at La Soumade

wines.description: Structured, dark Rasteau from Domaine La Soumade; Grenache-dominant with cocoa, iron, and herb complexity.

wines.name: Domaine La Soumade Rasteau Vin Doux Naturel
wines.oak_regime: Extended ageing in old demi-muids; oxidative style

taste.summary: A traditional oxidative-style Rasteau VDN; Grenache mutage produces rich, sweet wine with fig, chocolate, and coffee complexity. Excellent with dark chocolate and blue cheese.

pairings.why: Classic VDN-chocolate pairing; fig and coffee notes echo the bittersweet chocolate.
Sweet VDN with salty, creamy blue cheese is a Southern French classic.
The wine's dried fruit and walnut character echoes the nut-and-fruit plate.

history.summary: Rasteau VDN has been produced in the village for centuries; the style involves adding neutral grape spirit to arrest fermentation (mutage). La Soumade's oxidative-aged Rasteau VDN is among the appellation's most traditional and characterful examples.

milestones.event: La Soumade begins producing Rasteau VDN as a distinct wine
Rasteau VDN receives its own protected designation within the Rasteau AOC

wines.description: Traditional oxidative Rasteau Vin Doux Naturel from Domaine La Soumade; rich, sweet Grenache with fig, chocolate, and coffee complexity.

wines.name: Domaine Gourt de Mautens IGP Vaucluse
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres and amphora; no new oak; biodynamic

taste.summary: Jerome Bressy's Gourt de Mautens declassified from Rasteau AOC to IGP Vaucluse to maintain multi-variety blends prohibited under the stricter AOC rules; the wine is widely regarded as one of the Southern Rhone's most distinctive and complex reds.

pairings.dish: Roasted leg of lamb with tapenade crust
pairings.why: The wine's complexity and mineral depth match the concentrated olive-herb crust.
Iron and dried herb complexity are a natural match for white truffle.

history.summary: Jerome Bressy farms biodynamically and uses ancient varieties including Counoise and Cinsault in large proportions. The decision to declassify from Rasteau AOC to IGP was deliberate, allowing more varietal freedom. The wine is sought after by natural wine collectors worldwide.

milestones.event: Jerome Bressy establishes Domaine Gourt de Mautens in Rasteau
Declassification to IGP Vaucluse to allow multi-variety blends outside AOC rules

wines.description: Biodynamic Southern Rhone red from Domaine Gourt de Mautens, declassified to IGP Vaucluse for varietal freedom; a cult wine of extraordinary complexity.

wines.name: Domaine du Pegau Gigondas
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; same philosophy as Chateauneuf

taste.summary: Domaine du Pegau's Gigondas carries the same traditional old-foudre philosophy as their Chateauneuf; structured, herb-driven, and deeply characterful.

pairings.dish: Roasted lamb with lavender honey glaze
pairings.why: Southern herb and cherry notes pair with lavender-glazed lamb.
Dense fruit and tobacco complexity match rich braised beef.

history.summary: Pegau extended their portfolio to Gigondas to offer a more accessible Southern Rhone red in the same traditional style. The wine is produced from purchased old-vine Grenache in Gigondas, vinified and aged in old foudres.

milestones.event: Domaine du Pegau Gigondas first produced

wines.description: Domaine du Pegau's Gigondas; traditional old-foudre style, structured and herb-driven.

wines.name: Domaine Brusset Gigondas Les Hauts de Montmirail
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The top Gigondas from Brusset; sourced from Les Hauts de Montmirail, a high-altitude parcel producing concentrated, mineral Grenache with age-worthy structure.

pairings.why: Structured, earthy Gigondas is a natural match for traditional lamb.
Iron and leather notes complement gamey wild boar.

history.summary: The Brusset family's Gigondas comes from the Dentelles de Montmirail, the jagged limestone cliffs that dominate the appellation's eastern boundary. Les Hauts de Montmirail is the highest parcel in the Brusset Gigondas holdings.

milestones.event: Les Hauts de Montmirail first produced as a distinct single-parcel Gigondas

wines.description: Top Gigondas from Domaine Brusset, sourced from the high-altitude Les Hauts de Montmirail parcel; structured and mineral.

wines.name: Domaine Brusset Le Grand Montmirail Gigondas
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres

taste.summary: The approachable mid-tier Gigondas from Brusset; accessible and herb-accented with the characteristic Grenache warmth of the Dentelles de Montmirail.

pairings.why: Medium-bodied Gigondas is ideal with grilled pork sausages.
Herb notes and ripe fruit match well with lamb burgers.

history.summary: Le Grand Montmirail is Brusset's mid-tier Gigondas, named for the Dentelles de Montmirail range that frames the appellation. More approachable than Les Hauts de Montmirail.

milestones.event: Le Grand Montmirail Gigondas established at Domaine Brusset

wines.description: Mid-tier Gigondas from Domaine Brusset; approachable, herb-accented, and fruit-forward.

wines.name: Domaine Brusset Gigondas Tradition
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres

taste.summary: Entry-level, lightest and most approachable Gigondas from Brusset; fresh fruit, soft tannins, and herb character for everyday drinking.

pairings.why: Fresh, light Grenache is ideal for charcuterie without overpowering.

pairings.dish: Picnic with mixed olives and bread
pairings.why: Fresh herb notes and light tannins work well for informal outdoor eating.

history.summary: The entry-level Gigondas Tradition from Domaine Brusset; lighter and simpler than the single-parcel cuvees but offering the appellation's characteristic freshness and herb notes.

milestones.event: Gigondas Tradition established as the entry point in the Brusset range

wines.description: Entry-level Gigondas from Domaine Brusset; fresh, light, and herb-accented for everyday drinking.

wines.name: Domaine Brusset Cairanne
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: Estate Cairanne from Domaine Brusset; dark, cocoa-accented, and herb-driven from one of the Southern Rhone's most reliable Grenache appellations.

pairings.dish: Lamb stew with vegetables and herbs
pairings.why: Generous Grenache-Syrah matches rustic Provencal lamb stew.
Cocoa and dark fruit notes pair well with duck charcuterie.

history.summary: Domaine Brusset is headquartered in Cairanne, where the family farms old Grenache and Syrah across the village's clay-limestone soils. Cairanne was elevated to full AOC status in 2016.

milestones.event: Brusset Cairanne first produced as the estate red of the home appellation
Cairanne elevated from Cotes du Rhone Villages to full Cairanne AOC

wines.description: Estate Cairanne from Domaine Brusset; dark, cocoa-accented, and herb-driven from the home appellation.

wines.name: Domaine Brusset Cairanne Les Hauts de Montmirail Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres and used demi-muids

taste.summary: The prestige Cairanne from Brusset; higher Syrah content adds iron and structure, and the high-altitude parcels produce more concentrated, mineral fruit than the estate cuvee.

pairings.dish: Grilled lamb rack with black olive crust
pairings.why: Olive and iron notes echo the wine's savoury mineral depth.
Concentrated, structured Cairanne handles rich grilled beef excellently.

history.summary: Brusset's prestige Cairanne, from higher-altitude parcels near the Dentelles de Montmirail limestone escarpment. More structured and age-worthy than the estate Cairanne.

milestones.event: Cairanne Les Hauts de Montmirail first produced from high-altitude parcels

wines.description: Prestige Cairanne from Domaine Brusset from high-altitude parcels; structured, mineral, and Syrah-accented.

wines.name: Domaine de la Presidente Cairanne
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres

taste.summary: Approachable, reliable Cairanne from one of the appellation's well-established estates; generous fruit, good structure, and excellent value.

pairings.why: Warm dark fruit and structure work with rich, braised beef.
Herb-accented Grenache is a natural match for skewered lamb.

history.summary: Domaine de la Presidente is one of the larger and more traditional estates in Cairanne, founded by the Aubert family. The estate produces wines from Cairanne, Rasteau, and Cotes du Rhone.

milestones.event: La Presidente estate established in Cairanne by the Aubert family

wines.description: Approachable, generous Cairanne from Domaine de la Presidente; reliable value from one of the appellation's established estates.

wines.name: Domaine de la Presidente Rasteau
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres

taste.summary: Structured Rasteau from La Presidente; iron, chocolate, and herb notes on a firm Grenache backbone. Excellent value for the appellation.

pairings.dish: Grilled lamb cutlets with herb butter
pairings.why: Classic Southern Rhone Grenache with herb-finished lamb.
Mineral iron notes complement mushroom and beef combinations.

history.summary: Domaine de la Presidente also farms parcels in Rasteau, producing a structured, iron-accented red from the appellation's clay-limestone soils.

milestones.event: La Presidente Rasteau first produced from the estate's Rasteau parcels

wines.description: Structured Rasteau from Domaine de la Presidente; iron, chocolate, and herb notes on Grenache. Excellent value.

wines.name: Domaine La Soumade Cairanne
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres

taste.summary: A fruit-driven Cairanne from the Romero family; approachable, generous, and herb-accented with Grenache-dominant warmth.

pairings.why: Generous Grenache warmth and herb notes match rustic sausage dishes.

pairings.dish: Provencal pizza with tomatoes and herbs
pairings.why: Herb and fruit notes pair naturally with herb-topped pizza.

history.summary: La Soumade produces a range of wines from the Rasteau-Cairanne area. The Cairanne is their approachable everyday red from the neighbouring appellation.

milestones.event: La Soumade Cairanne first produced

wines.description: Approachable, fruit-forward Cairanne from Domaine La Soumade; Grenache warmth and herb character.

wines.name: Tardieu-Laurent Rasteau
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in used French oak barrique

taste.summary: Negociant Rasteau from Tardieu-Laurent; concentrated, barrel-aged, and structured, showing the appellation's characteristic iron and dark fruit character.

pairings.dish: Leg of lamb with garlic and rosemary
pairings.why: Classic Southern Rhone herb-and-lamb pairing; concentrated Grenache handles the richness.
Firm tannins and mineral iron pair naturally with hard aged cheese.

history.summary: Tardieu-Laurent's Rasteau is sourced from old-vine Grenache parcels in the village by negociant Michel Tardieu, following the house style of barrel ageing and careful selection.

milestones.event: Tardieu-Laurent Rasteau first produced

wines.description: Negociant Rasteau from Tardieu-Laurent; concentrated, barrel-aged, and mineral from old-vine Grenache.

wines.name: Domaine de la Mordoree La Dame Rousse Tavel
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel only; no oak

taste.summary: The reference Tavel from La Mordoree; a full-bodied, structured dry rose with vibrant red fruit, garrigue freshness, and remarkable depth for the style.

pairings.why: Full-bodied dry Tavel rose is the traditional Provencal accompaniment to fish stew.

pairings.dish: Grilled squid with garlic and herbs
pairings.why: High acidity and mineral freshness cut through squid richness.
Herb notes and red fruit character are natural companions to the classic salad.

pairings.dish: Tapenade on grilled bread with goat cheese
pairings.why: Mineral freshness and red fruit balance the salt of tapenade and tang of cheese.

history.summary: La Dame Rousse is the flagship Tavel of Domaine de la Mordoree, sourced from the estate's Tavel parcels which are among the oldest and most prestigious in the appellation. Tavel is the only French AOC dedicated exclusively to rose wine.

milestones.event: La Dame Rousse established as the flagship Tavel at La Mordoree
La Mordoree's Tavel consistently recognised as among the best of the appellation

wines.description: The reference Tavel from Domaine de la Mordoree; full-bodied, mineral, and vibrant dry rose.

wines.name: Chateau d'Aqueria Tavel
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; no oak

taste.summary: A classic, benchmark Tavel from one of the appellation's oldest estates; fresh, dry, and mineral with vibrant red fruit and herb notes. The reliable reference for the style.

pairings.why: Classic Provencal combination; mineral dry rose and oily fish.
Herb notes and fresh acidity complement the vegetable-rich dish.
Fresh, dry Tavel is the Provencal aperitif rose par excellence.

history.summary: Chateau d'Aqueria is one of the oldest and most storied estates in the Tavel appellation, with documented production dating to the early 20th century. The de Bez family owns the estate today. The Tavel AOC, established in 1936, is France's only AOC dedicated solely to rose wine.

milestones.event: Documented production at Chateau d'Aqueria in the Tavel AOC
Tavel receives its own AOC, the first rose-only appellation in France

wines.description: Classic benchmark Tavel from Chateau d'Aqueria; fresh, dry, and mineral from one of the appellation's oldest estates.

wines.name: Domaine de la Mordoree La Reine des Bois Lirac Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The Lirac counterpart to La Mordoree's Chateauneuf; similar Mourvedre-accented depth and garrigue character but at a more accessible price from the neighbouring appellation.

pairings.why: Herb-driven dark fruit pairs naturally with grilled lamb and vegetable.
Dark fruit and leather notes complement duck and the fruity sauce.

history.summary: La Mordoree's original home is in Tavel, and the estate also owns Lirac parcels across the Rhone from Chateauneuf. The Reine des Bois Lirac is the red expression from these old Grenache and Mourvedre vines.

milestones.event: Reine des Bois Lirac rouge first produced from estate parcels

wines.description: Red Lirac from Domaine de la Mordoree; Mourvedre-accented, herb-driven, and built for moderate ageing.

wines.name: Domaine de la Mordoree La Reine des Bois Lirac Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres and stainless steel; no new oak

taste.summary: Fresh, aromatic white Lirac from La Mordoree; multi-variety Grenache Blanc-led blend with white blossom, peach, and herb character.

pairings.why: Anise and herb notes echo grilled fennel; fresh acidity suits white fish.
White Lirac is a natural companion for Provencal soupe de poisson.

history.summary: The white Reine des Bois Lirac is a multi-variety blend drawn from La Mordoree's Lirac white-variety plantings; fresh and aromatic, it is the everyday white counterpart to the estate's richer white Chateauneuf.

milestones.event: White Lirac first produced at La Mordoree

wines.description: Fresh, aromatic white Lirac from Domaine de la Mordoree; Grenache Blanc-led with white blossom and herb character.

wines.name: Domaine de la Mordoree Lirac Rose
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; no oak

taste.summary: An easy, fresh, dry Lirac rose from La Mordoree; lighter-framed and more quaffable than the Tavel, with bright cherry fruit and herb freshness.

pairings.why: Light rose with herb freshness pairs naturally with herb-roasted chicken.
Light fruity rose with fresh herb notes is ideal for tomato dishes.

history.summary: La Mordoree's Lirac rose is lighter and more accessible than their Tavel, drawing on Grenache and Cinsault from Lirac parcels for a fresh, easy summer wine.

milestones.event: Lirac rose first produced at La Mordoree

wines.description: Light, fresh, dry Lirac rose from Domaine de la Mordoree; quaffable and herb-accented for summer drinking.

wines.name: Domaine de Coyeux Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; no oak; served young and cold

taste.summary: A fresh, floral Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise from Coyeux; delicate apricot and peach blossom with natural sweetness and clean acidity. An ideal dessert aperitif or pairing for fresh fruit.

pairings.why: The wine's apricot and honey sweetness complements fresh strawberry desserts.
Sweet Muscat with salty, fatty foie gras is a southern French classic.
The wine's stone-fruit and floral character echoes melon's sweetness.

history.summary: Domaine de Coyeux is a family estate in Beaumes-de-Venise, farming old Muscat Blanc a Petits Grains on the village's clay-limestone soils below the Dentelles de Montmirail. The Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise AOC is one of the oldest VDN appellations in France.

milestones.event: Coyeux establishes its Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise production
Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise AOC established as one of France's first VDN appellations

wines.description: Fresh, floral Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise from Domaine de Coyeux; delicate apricot and peach blossom sweetness.

wines.name: Domaine de Coyeux Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise Reserve
wines.oak_regime: Short period in old demi-muids

taste.summary: The Reserve Muscat from Coyeux; more concentrated and richer than the standard cuvee, with brief time in old oak adding almond and caramel notes.

pairings.why: Caramelised apple tart is a natural partner for the wine's apricot and caramel.
Sweet Muscat with cream custard is a classic southern French dessert pairing.

history.summary: The Reserve Muscat was introduced by Coyeux to offer a richer, more concentrated alternative to the standard cuvee. Brief time in old wood adds complexity without masking the grape's aromatic purity.

milestones.event: Coyeux Reserve Muscat first produced with brief old-oak ageing

wines.description: Reserve Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise from Domaine de Coyeux; richer and more concentrated than the standard cuvee.

wines.name: Domaine des Bernardins Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; no oak

taste.summary: One of Beaumes-de-Venise's most traditional producers; the Muscat from Bernardins is floral, perfumed, and delicate with rose water and lychee notes from high-altitude Muscat vines.

pairings.dish: Fresh fruit salad with citrus and mint
pairings.why: Floral, lychee-accented sweetness lifts the freshness of fruit salad.
Fragrant Muscat complements delicate patisserie perfectly.

history.summary: The Aubert family's Domaine des Bernardins has produced Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise since the 1960s from high-altitude vines on the rocky slopes of the Dentelles de Montmirail. One of the most traditionally styled estates in the appellation.

milestones.event: Bernardins Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise first produced by the Aubert family

wines.description: Floral, delicate Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise from Domaine des Bernardins; rose water, lychee, and apricot character from high-altitude vines.

wines.name: Domaine des Bernardins Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise Vieux Millesimes
wines.oak_regime: Extended ageing in old demi-muids; oxidative

taste.summary: An aged, oxidative Muscat with extraordinary complexity; dried citrus, almond, saffron, and fig from extended old-wood ageing. A Southern Rhone rarity with the complexity of a great Sauternes.

pairings.why: Dried apricot and almond richness mirrors the wine's deep dried-fruit sweetness.
Rich oxidative sweetness contrasts with salty, pungent blue cheese.

history.summary: Bernardins introduced their aged Muscat offering in 1985, allowing select vintages to oxidise slowly in old demi-muids. The result is a rare Southern Rhone dessert wine that can rival aged Sauternes or Banyuls in complexity.

milestones.event: First Vieux Millesimes Muscat aged in oxidative style at Bernardins

wines.description: Aged oxidative Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise from Domaine des Bernardins; extraordinary complexity with dried citrus, almond, saffron, and fig.

wines.name: Famille Perrin Reserve Cotes du Rhone Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The benchmark for value Cotes du Rhone; the Perrin Reserve is fruit-driven, herb-accented, and reliably consistent for everyday drinking.

pairings.why: Medium-bodied, fruit-forward CDR is a natural pairing for grilled chicken.
Fresh herb notes and soft tannins pair well with simple tomato pizza.
Accessible Grenache-led CDR works well with a casual cheese spread.

history.summary: The Famille Perrin Reserve range is the entry-tier wine of the Beaucastel family's branded range. Sourced from multiple CDR appellations, it is one of the most recognised value Cotes du Rhone labels internationally.

milestones.event: Famille Perrin Reserve established as the accessible entry-tier Cotes du Rhone

wines.description: Famille Perrin Reserve Cotes du Rhone; fruit-forward, herb-accented, and a benchmark value CDR.

wines.name: La Vieille Ferme Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; no oak

taste.summary: La Vieille Ferme is the ultimate everyday Southern Rhone; light, fresh, herb-scented, and designed for uncomplicated drinking. One of the best-selling French wines globally.

pairings.why: Simple red fruit and herb character is perfect for tomato-based pasta.
Light, fresh, and fruit-forward for casual grilled food.

history.summary: La Vieille Ferme is the Perrin family's everyday brand, launched in 1970 to provide an accessible, good-value Provence-adjacent red. It draws from Ventoux AOC fruit and is now one of the most-exported French wines.

milestones.event: La Vieille Ferme brand launched by the Perrin family
La Vieille Ferme becomes one of France's most-exported wines by volume

wines.description: La Vieille Ferme, the Perrin family's beloved everyday wine; light, fresh, herb-scented, and one of France's most-sold reds.

wines.name: Domaine de la Janasse Cotes du Rhone Terre d'Argile
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: A step above the average CDR; Janasse's entry red shows the estate's characteristic depth and herb character at an accessible price.

pairings.why: Warm, fruity Grenache matches spiced lamb and cooling dairy.
Herb notes and warm fruit complement Provencal grilled vegetables.

history.summary: The Janasse Cotes du Rhone Terre d'Argile is sourced from the estate's younger-vine and clay-rich parcels outside the Chateauneuf-du-Pape appellation. 'Terre d'Argile' ('Clay Soil') reflects the terroir of this entry-level cuvee.

milestones.event: Terre d'Argile CDR established at La Janasse

wines.description: Entry-level Cotes du Rhone from Domaine de la Janasse; herb-accented and fruit-forward, a step above the average CDR.

wines.name: Domaine de la Presidente Cotes du Rhone
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: Easy, fruit-forward Cotes du Rhone from the Aubert family; light, approachable, and reliably consistent for everyday use.

pairings.why: Light, fresh CDR is the natural partner for Provencal charcuterie.
Soft tannins and fresh fruit match grilled chicken without overpowering.

history.summary: The entry Cotes du Rhone from La Presidente, sourced from CDR parcels across the northern Vaucluse. Simple, reliable, and well-priced.

milestones.event: La Presidente CDR established as the entry tier in the range

wines.description: Light, approachable Cotes du Rhone from Domaine de la Presidente; fruit-forward and everyday-friendly.

wines.name: Domaine Brusset Cotes du Rhone Villages Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: Reliable CDR Villages from Brusset; a step up from the basic CDR with more concentration and earthy depth from the estate's Cairanne-area fruit.

pairings.dish: Grilled chicken with olives and tomatoes
pairings.why: Herb-and-dark-fruit CDR is a natural partner for Provencal chicken.
Dark fruit and herb notes echo the olive, anchovy, and caper sauce.

history.summary: The Brusset Cotes du Rhone Villages is sourced from the estate's lower-tier parcels in the Cairanne area, offering good value with characteristic Southern Rhone fruit and herb character.

milestones.event: Brusset CDR Villages established as the entry wine of the range

wines.description: Reliable CDR Villages from Domaine Brusset; fruit-forward with Southern Rhone herb and mineral character.

wines.name: Domaine de la Janasse Chateauneuf-du-Pape Blanc
wines.oak_regime: Old demi-muids and foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: A generous white Chateauneuf from La Janasse; aromatic, broad-textured, and herb-accented with white stone fruit and the estate's characteristic Provencal freshness.

pairings.why: Full-bodied white Chateauneuf is the traditional Provencal pairing for the classic fish stew.

pairings.dish: Grilled sea bream with herb crust
pairings.why: Herb notes and full body complement white fish with Provencal herbs.
Anise and mineral notes cut through the garlic richness of aioli.

history.summary: The white Chateauneuf from La Janasse was introduced in 1998 from Grenache Blanc, Roussanne, and Clairette vines at the estate. It offers access to the white Chateauneuf style at a more approachable price than Beaucastel or La Nerthe Clos de Beauvenir.

milestones.event: White Chateauneuf-du-Pape first produced at Domaine de la Janasse

wines.description: White Chateauneuf-du-Pape from La Janasse; generous, broad-textured, and herb-accented from Grenache Blanc, Roussanne, and Clairette.

wines.name: Chateau d'Aqueria Lirac Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The red Lirac from Aqueria; good value, herb-driven, and fruit-forward with the estate's characteristic Provencal warmth. Lirac at its most accessible and traditional.

pairings.why: Warm, herb-driven Grenache is a natural match for spiced lamb sausage.
Earthy herb notes complement olive-based tapenade.
Medium-bodied Lirac is approachable with herb-roasted chicken.

history.summary: Chateau d'Aqueria has been producing red Lirac for much of the 20th century from old Grenache and Syrah vines across the Lirac appellation on the right bank of the Rhone. The Lirac AOC was established in 1945.

milestones.event: Red Lirac production documented at Chateau d'Aqueria
Lirac AOC established on the right bank of the Rhone

wines.description: Classic red Lirac from Chateau d'Aqueria; herb-driven, fruit-forward, and excellent value from the right-bank appellation.

wines.name: Tardieu-Laurent Cotes du Rhone Vieilles Vignes
wines.oak_regime: Used French oak barrique; 12 months

taste.summary: Tardieu-Laurent's CDR entry; old-vine Grenache and Syrah in the house style ,  more structured and concentrated than typical CDR, with barrel influence adding spice and depth.

pairings.why: Concentrated CDR with barrel spice is a natural match for herb-grilled lamb.
Dark fruit and smoked herb notes complement rich duck rillettes.

history.summary: The entry-level CDR from negociant Tardieu-Laurent, sourced from old Grenache and Syrah parcels across the Southern Rhone. More ambitious than the average CDR, with barrel influence and high ABV from over-ripe southern fruit.

milestones.event: Tardieu-Laurent CDR Vieilles Vignes first produced

wines.description: Old-vine Cotes du Rhone from Tardieu-Laurent; more concentrated and barrel-aged than most CDR, showing the house's structured style.

wines.name: Domaine Charvin Cotes du Rhone
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak; organic

taste.summary: Laurent Charvin's CDR carries the estate's characteristic elegance and mineral precision into an everyday format; lighter-framed but aromatic and precise.

pairings.why: Fresh, elegant Grenache with high acidity is a natural with herb-roasted chicken.
The wine's mineral freshness and red fruit work with the saltiness of anchovies.

history.summary: Domaine Charvin's CDR is produced from organic Grenache planted on sandy-limestone soils outside the Chateauneuf appellation. It shares the estate's characteristic mineral precision at an accessible price.

milestones.event: Charvin Cotes du Rhone established as the estate entry wine

wines.description: Organic Cotes du Rhone from Laurent Charvin; elegant, mineral, and precise with fresh red fruit.

wines.name: Domaine de Marcoux Cotes du Rhone
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak; biodynamic

taste.summary: Biodynamic Cotes du Rhone from Marcoux; the estate's characteristic aromatic precision and silky texture in an everyday-drinking format.

pairings.why: Silky, herb-accented Grenache is an ideal match for grilled lamb skewers.
Lavender and herb notes echo the Provencal vegetable dish.

history.summary: The Marcoux CDR is produced biodynamically from Grenache and Mourvedre on the estate's lighter parcels outside the Chateauneuf appellation. It carries the estate's characteristic aromatic elegance at a fraction of the Chateauneuf price.

milestones.event: Biodynamic CDR first produced at Domaine de Marcoux

wines.description: Biodynamic Cotes du Rhone from Domaine de Marcoux; aromatic, silky, and precise in the estate's elegant style.

wines.name: Domaine du Cayron Cotes du Rhone
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: An unpretentious, honest CDR from the Faraud family in their characteristic old-foudre, no-intervention style; fresh cherry, herbs, and light minerality for everyday drinking.

pairings.why: Light, fresh Grenache is ideal for simple egg-and-cheese dishes.
Herbal freshness and light fruit pair perfectly with Provencal charcuterie.

history.summary: Domaine du Cayron's CDR is the entry wine of the Faraud family estate, sourced from their younger-vine CDR parcels in the same traditional style as their acclaimed Gigondas.

milestones.event: Cayron CDR established as the Faraud family entry wine

wines.description: Honest, traditional Cotes du Rhone from Domaine du Cayron; fresh cherry, herbs, and light minerality in the old-foudre style.

wines.name: Domaine du Pegau Cotes du Rhone de Mon Pere
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: A generous, warm CDR from Pegau; more concentrated and structured than most CDR, carrying the estate's southern warmth and herb character in an everyday format.

pairings.why: Warm, spiced Grenache CDR is a natural match for North-African-inflected grilled meat.
Full-bodied CDR handles richer pizza toppings with ease.

history.summary: 'De Mon Pere' ('from my father') is Pegau's CDR, produced in the same traditional old-foudre style as the Chateauneuf. It offers early access to the Pegau character at a CDR price.

milestones.event: Cotes du Rhone de Mon Pere launched at Domaine du Pegau

wines.description: Generous, warm Cotes du Rhone from Domaine du Pegau in the estate's traditional old-foudre style.

wines.name: Domaine du Vieux Telegraphe La Croix des Bois Cotes du Rhone
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The CDR entry point for the Brunier family; organic, herb-accented, and precisely made in the characteristic Vieux Telegraphe style at an accessible price.

pairings.why: Herb-scented Grenache CDR is a natural match for simple grilled lamb.
Red fruit and herb freshness pair well with earthy lentil dishes.

history.summary: La Croix des Bois is the Brunier family's Cotes du Rhone, sourced from organic parcels below the La Crau plateau. It carries the estate's commitment to organic farming and old-foudre winemaking at an everyday price.

milestones.event: La Croix des Bois CDR first produced by the Brunier family

wines.description: Organic Cotes du Rhone from the Brunier family of Vieux Telegraphe; herb-accented and precisely made at an accessible price.

wines.name: Coudoulet de Beaucastel Cotes du Rhone
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak; biodynamic

taste.summary: The CDR from the Beaucastel estate, produced from a parcel just outside the Chateauneuf appellation boundary; it shares the estate's biodynamic farming, Mourvedre content, and old-foudre ageing in a more accessible format.

pairings.why: Mourvedre-accented Coudoulet is a natural match for slow-braised lamb and bean stew.
Full-bodied with good tannins; a reliable CDR for grilled beef.
Iron and herb notes complement the duck and pork of this Languedoc classic.

history.summary: Coudoulet de Beaucastel is produced from a parcel called Coudoulet de Beaucastel, located directly outside the Chateauneuf-du-Pape appellation boundary but farmed identically to the Beaucastel estate. First produced in 1974, it has become one of France's most famous second labels and a benchmark CDR.

milestones.event: Coudoulet de Beaucastel first produced from the parcel outside the CdP boundary

wines.description: Coudoulet de Beaucastel, the famous CDR from the Beaucastel parcel outside Chateauneuf; biodynamic, Mourvedre-accented, and one of France's great value reds.

wines.name: Le Sang des Cailloux Vacqueyras Rouge
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak; organic

taste.summary: The estate red Vacqueyras from Sang des Cailloux; a benchmark expression of the appellation with garrigue, mineral depth, and good structure.

pairings.why: Classic Southern Rhone match; olive and herb notes echo in the wine.
Dark fruit and tobacco notes complement duck and fig.

history.summary: The estate red Vacqueyras of Sang des Cailloux, the domaine's entry red, separate from the premium Cuvee Lopy. Serge Ferigoule farms organically and vinifies traditionally.

milestones.event: Sang des Cailloux estate red Vacqueyras first produced

wines.description: Estate red Vacqueyras from Le Sang des Cailloux; benchmark appellation character, garrigue-driven and mineral.

wines.name: Chateau La Nerthe Les Pallieroudas Cotes du Rhone
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: The accessible CDR from La Nerthe; organic, herb-accented, and approachable with the estate's characteristic Southern Rhone warmth.

pairings.why: Herb-forward CDR matches Provencal vegetables and garlic dip.
Fresh fruit and herb character pairs naturally with Southern French charcuterie.

history.summary: Les Pallieroudas is the CDR entry wine from Chateau La Nerthe, sourced from the estate's CDR-classified parcels. 'Pallieroudas' refers to the sandy galets found in this part of the property.

milestones.event: Les Pallieroudas CDR established at Chateau La Nerthe

wines.description: Accessible organic CDR from Chateau La Nerthe; herb-accented and approachable in the estate's characteristic Southern Rhone style.

wines.name: Domaine Raspail-Ay Cotes du Rhone
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: Entry-level CDR from the traditional Raspail-Ay estate; fresh cherry, herbs, and the estate's characteristic dry mineral finish in an everyday format.

pairings.why: Light Grenache CDR with fresh herb notes is ideal with a classic herbed omelette.
Easy-drinking CDR pairs naturally with French dried sausage.

history.summary: The Raspail-Ay CDR is the entry wine of the Ay family estate, sourced from their CDR parcels in the same traditional no-filtration, old-foudre style as the Gigondas.

milestones.event: Raspail-Ay CDR established as the entry tier of the range

wines.description: Traditional CDR from Domaine Raspail-Ay; fresh cherry, herbs, and dry mineral finish in an honest everyday format.

wines.name: Domaine Santa Duc Gigondas Aux Lieux-Dits
wines.oak_regime: Old foudres; no new oak

taste.summary: A multi-parcel Gigondas from Santa Duc that blends several specific lieux-dits; more structured and mineral than the estate Gigondas, bridging toward the Prestige des Hautes Garrigues in complexity.

pairings.dish: Slow-roasted shoulder of lamb with garlic and thyme
pairings.why: Structured, herb-mineral Gigondas is a natural with slow-roasted lamb.
Cedar and herb notes complement wild mushroom dishes.

history.summary: Aux Lieux-Dits is Santa Duc's intermediate Gigondas, blending grapes from several named parcels in the appellation. First produced in 2000, it sits between the estate classique and the top Prestige des Hautes Garrigues.

milestones.event: Aux Lieux-Dits first produced as a multi-parcel blended Gigondas at Santa Duc

wines.description: Multi-parcel Gigondas from Domaine Santa Duc; structured, mineral, and herb-driven from several named lieux-dits.
